title: #eng-oncall: 1768865409
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Dropping the runbook draft here for review before we flip paging on phase 1. Mainly want eyes on the decision tree + escalation expectations. Doc: https://confluence.redwood.dev/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR structure is:
1) Validate alert (real vs noise)
2) Classify: queueing vs runtime vs infra
3) Mitigate: rollback / disable canary / freeze
4) Escalate w/ a clean payload

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Skimmed. It’s close, but I want it to be more “do X then Y” and less narrative. Oncall at 3am won’t read paragraphs.

Aisha Bello: +1. The decision tree graphic is helpful but we should add explicit thresholds in each branch (even if they’re “see alert payload”). E.g. “prefill_p95 up but decode flat” etc.

Noah Patel: Looking now. One thing: runbook should say what to check in the canary payload first: model, region, tier, hardware_sku, runtime_build, kernel_id, scheduler_version.

Allison Grant: Also make sure links are consistent. In the alert we’ll have Grafana panel + trace explorer + baseline artifact link. Runbook should say “start from alert links, don’t go hunting.”

Rafael Mendes: Got it. I can rewrite top section as a checklist + move the narrative to “Background” at bottom.

Sofia Ivanova: In the classifier section, we should be explicit about queueing-time interpretation. We’ve seen folks confuse “end-to-end p95” with “runtime-only p95”.

Sofia Ivanova: Suggest a table like:
- If queue_ms_p95 ↑ and runtime_ms_p95 flat → capacity/scheduling/backpressure
- If runtime_ms_p95 ↑ and queue_ms flat → kernel/runtime regression
- If both ↑ → could be fleet saturation or routing shift

Omar El-Sayed: + add “batch_size distribution changed?” That’s a smoking gun for scheduler heuristics change or sudden traffic mix shift.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel escalation: please include when to page kernels vs file ticket. I don’t want kernel oncall spam for warn-only alerts.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. My proposal:
- Warn alert: post in #eng-runtime w/ context, no page
- Page alert: page SRE primary + runtime oncall; kernel oncall only if runtime_ms regression and kernel_id changed in same window

Aisha Bello: Can we put this in an explicit escalation matrix block? Like rows=severity, cols=teams.

Rafael Mendes: Yes. I’ll add “Escalation Matrix” section. Can someone confirm the runtime oncall alias? Is it @runtime-oncall or @serving-runtime-oncall?

Noah Patel: It’s @serving-runtime-oncall (routes to Noah/Sofia/Hiro rotation).

Allison Grant: Make sure we don’t assume access to internal dashboards. Oncall doc should mention “if links 403, ask #eng-platform-oncall or open INT ticket.” We’ve had that bite folks.

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra/fleet angle: runbook should include “check recent GPU fleet changes in region” + “check routing policy change / weight shift”. We can link to Fleet Change Log.

Vanessa Ortiz: We have a canonical dashboard for that: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/fleet-changes (needs VPN). Add it as optional step.

Connor O'Brien: For mitigation: the runbook should state what actions are safe without release eng approval.
- Safe: pause rollout gate / hold further regions
- Needs approval: rollback runtime build, change kernel flag, change routing weights

Sean Gallagher: + include the “disable switch” for canary itself. If canary is causing load or alert storm, oncall should know how to set canary.enabled=false per region.

Rafael Mendes: Do we want oncall to disable canary in prod directly? Or only SRE can?

Sean Gallagher: SRE oncall can, but the runbook should say “only if overhead > budget OR alert storm confirmed.” Otherwise we lose signal.

Aisha Bello: Can you add a quick check for overhead? Something like “perf-canary GPU budget panel should be <0.5% per region” with the exact Grafana panel link.

Noah Patel: Another thing: the “Validate alert” step should include minimum sample size. E.g., if observed_n < min_samples, it’s likely noise. People tend to overreact.

Allison Grant: Yes, and include “anti-flap is already applied” so oncall doesn’t manually wait 30m unless the rule indicates it.

Hiro Tanaka: Small but important: when escalating to kernels, include kernel_id + arch + seq_len bucket if we have it. Otherwise we can’t reproduce.

Omar El-Sayed: We have seq_len_bucket and prompt_shape_id tags in canary metrics. Runbook should explicitly say to paste those into the thread.

Sofia Ivanova: On the decision tree, I’d also add a branch for telemetry pipeline delay. We had one case where baseline fetch lagged and the alert fired “late” with confusing timestamps.

Rafael Mendes: Good call. I’ll add: “If dashboards show flat but alert claims regression, check telemetry ingestion delay panel / statuspage.”

Connor O'Brien: Rollback guidance: runbook should mention when to revert runtime build vs when to revert config (scheduler knobs). We can’t always roll back runtime quickly.

Noah Patel: Maybe:
- If regression aligns with runtime_build change → rollback runtime (if within canary rollout window)
- If aligns with config rollout (batching heuristic) → flip feature flag / config revert
- If aligns with fleet change → engage infra, consider draining bad nodes

Sean Gallagher: + include comms expectation: for a page-level sustained regression on top models, open incident in #incidents and update every 30m. Warn-level stays in #eng-runtime thread.

Aisha Bello: Please add explicit “Definition of sustained” (e.g. 2 consecutive windows / 20 min). That’s currently implicit.

Rafael Mendes: Ack. I’ll incorporate these edits today and repost for final sign-off.

Allison Grant: When you repost, can you also add a “Copy/paste template” for the incident thread? Like:
Model:
Region:
Tier:
Hardware:
Observed:
Baseline:
Runtime build:
Kernel id:
Queue vs runtime breakdown:
Mitigation taken:

Rafael Mendes: Yep, will do.

Hiro Tanaka: Once updated, tag me on the kernel escalation section. Want to make sure it’s crisp.

Sean Gallagher: Same for SRE actions. I want to ensure we’re not asking oncall to do anything risky without guardrails.

Rafael Mendes: Sounds good. Targeting EOD. :thumbsup: (will post updated link in this thread)

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: 

Noah Patel: :thumbsup:
text:

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR structure is:
1) Validate alert (real vs noise)
2) Classify: queueing vs runtime vs infra
3) Mitigate: rollback / disable canary / freeze
4) Escalate w/ a clean payload

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Skimmed. It’s close, but I want it to be more “do X then Y” and less narrative. Oncall at 3am won’t read paragraphs.

title: #eng-oncall: 1768653900
preview:
Tomáš Novák: coming out of gameday + the rehearsal threads, we need to decide what runbook edits are *blocking* before we expand automation to more routes/models. can we align here so we don’t ship more confusion to oncall?

Rafael Mendes: +1. oncall feedback was basically “runbooks don’t match what the controller actually does now”. we should pick a minimal set that must be correct before we flip any more tenants.

Miles Thornton: agree. I’d separate into (A) correctness/safety (blocking), (B) usability (soon), (C) nice-to-have.

Kira Thompson: I can take edits but need crisp scope. right now we have 3 docs that point at each other and disagree on who approves what.

Tomáš Novák: exactly. proposed runbooks in scope for this thread:
- routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook (controller ops)
- hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook (oncall procedure)
- gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure (dedicated + infra)

Mina Haddad: add routing policy “what changes when” too. oncall keeps asking “is it DNS? is it policy engine?”

Allison Grant: and dashboards spec needs a “during event: where do i look first” section. but we can probably link it vs duplicating.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s list the blockers.

Miles Thornton: Blocker #1: single decision tree for “automation allowed vs manual” + required corroboration. right now Hosted API runbook says “if 5xx > X then failover” which is not true anymore (needs reachability or burn corroboration per ADR-042).

Tomáš Novák: yes. oncall was ready to failover on a single noisy 5xx spike in eu-west. need “DO NOT FAILOVER on isolated 5xx if perf-canary reachability is green + control plane healthy” spelled out.

Victor Alvarez: +1. add explicit network checks before blaming region: perf-canary cross-region probe panel + cloud provider status page link. Also note that DNS TTL changes mean clients may not move instantly.

Peter Holtz: Blocker #2: circuit breaker / retry budget guidance during failover. we saw thundering herd due to clients retrying immediately on 307/429. runbook should say: keep gateway retry budget low, prefer `retry-after`, and when to enable load shedding mode.

Rafael Mendes: yep. we need a “set expectations for error shape” section: 429 + Retry-After during overload is expected; it’s safer than timeouts.

Kira Thompson: i can add a “Customer impact expectations” box: what users will see (streaming disconnects, redirect, brief 429). But need final wording from runtime/gateway folks.

Devon Price: Blocker #3: incident-bot + comms steps. During the test, we weren’t sure when to post internal update vs status page note. runbook should say who triggers the comms playbook + what’s optional for planned tests.

Tomáš Novák: +1. also: who owns the “go/no-go” and who can hit the big red disable automation.

Rafael Mendes: for prod: oncall SRE (primary) can disable automation, Miles (or routing oncall) can override policy flips. Need this written.

Miles Thornton: Blocker #4: rollback semantics. oncall didn’t know whether rollback was automatic or manual and what the hold-down timer is. we need explicit “after failover: minimum hold-down N minutes; rollback only if X/Y/Z signals recover AND traffic is stable” so we don’t flap.

Mina Haddad: please also include the per-tenant safety limits (max flips/hour). oncall asked “why won’t it flip again?” and assumed it was broken.

Allison Grant: Blocker #5: dashboard links + “what good looks like” per signal. for each step in runbook, link the panel:
- reachability (perf-canary)
- 5xx burn + latency burn (SLO burn)
- capacity headroom
- policy flip/audit events
And add 1-2 bullets on interpreting each.

Kira Thompson: can we just hardcode the canonical dashboard URLs? or do we need environment-specific placeholders?

Allison Grant: put canonical + a note: “if in staging, switch env filter to staging-*”. we can keep it simple.

Victor Alvarez: also add the DNS/anycast constraints section as “FYI”: TTL 60s doesn’t mean all clients re-resolve. some SDKs cache longer.

Tomáš Novák: ok, summarizing blockers so far:
1) Decision tree + trigger corroboration + “do not failover” cases (ADR-042 aligned)
2) Circuit breaker / retry budget / load shedding mode + expected error shapes
3) Ownership + approvals + comms hooks (incident-bot/status)
4) Rollback semantics + hold-down timers + anti-flap / safety limits
5) Dashboards: direct links + interpretation guidance
Anything else truly blocking?

Rafael Mendes: Dedicated: blocker #6 is drain sequencing. The dedicated drain runbook currently starts with “drain pool” but doesn’t say “confirm routing has shifted tenant traffic” vs “drain will cause 5xx”. needs a safe order.

Miles Thornton: yes: “route -> verify -> drain -> verify -> scale”. plus explicit abort conditions.

Peter Holtz: and make it clear when drain automation is used vs manual commands. if it’s feature-flagged, say so.

Mina Haddad: also: add a tiny glossary. “region impaired” vs “partial degradation” vs “control plane degraded”. we had people talking past each other.

Tomáš Novák: glossary is nice, but is it blocking?

Rafael Mendes: borderline, but I’d include a 5-line definitions section at top of Hosted API runbook. it reduces mistakes.

Allison Grant: +1. definitions reduce wrong paging too.

Kira Thompson: ok, I’ll include a short Definitions section.

Devon Price: one more: audit trail. enterprise asks “show me what happened” (SUP-25444-ish). runbook should say where audit events live and how to export links for support.

Rafael Mendes: good call. put as “Post-event: collect artifacts” checklist.

Tomáš Novák: so priorities / order of operations:
P0 (must land before enabling automation for additional routes/models): blockers 1-6 + definitions + audit trail pointers.
P1: nicer troubleshooting playbooks (e.g., “controller says NO_ACTION, now what?”) + deeper client SDK guidance.

Miles Thornton: works. assign owners?

Kira Thompson: I can draft the runbook edits if I get reviewers and source of truth for hold-down timers + safety limits.

Mina Haddad: i’ll provide the routing/policy details (hold-down, anti-flap, safety limits) + what toggles exist.

Peter Holtz: i’ll write the gateway/load shedding / retry budget section + expected 429/503/307 behavior.

Allison Grant: i’ll drop the canonical dashboard links + quick “reading signals” bullets.

Devon Price: I’ll add comms + incident-bot steps + what gets auto-posted vs manual.

Victor Alvarez: I’ll add the network checks and DNS caveats section + links to the networking notes.

Rafael Mendes: i’ll own final review for “oncall correctness” + publish checklist. target: edits merged by EOW so we can unblock next rollout wave.

Tomáš Novák: perfect. can we agree: no expansion to more routes/models until the P0 runbooks are updated and reviewed by SRE + routing + infra?

Rafael Mendes: yes. :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: also let’s add a short “runbook version” note in the rollout checklist so we can confirm prod oncall is looking at the right doc.

Kira Thompson: ack. i’ll start a doc PR / change log section so we can point to what changed after gameday.

Allison Grant: please ping me when you want dashboard links; i’ll make sure they’re stable + include the annotation panel callout.

Tomáš Novák: thanks all. i’ll post a mini checklist in-thread once Kira has the draft so reviewers know what to look for.

title: #eng-infra: 1768469814
preview:
Victor Alvarez: heads up, we’re proposing DNS TTL reductions for regional failover (Hosted + some Dedicated endpoints). Want a quick risk assessment here before we merge the tf changes.

Victor Alvarez: current state (most public records): TTL=60s (a few are 300s still). Proposal: drop to 20s for the failover-weighted records, keep everything else at 60s.

Irene Choi: 20s is… aggressive but maybe fine. Can you clarify which records exactly? `api.redwoodinference.com` is behind the geo/latency policy right?

Victor Alvarez: yep. Specifically:
- `api.redwoodinference.com` (latency/weighted policy)
- `stream.api.redwoodinference.com`
- `dedicated.<region>.redwoodinference.com` NOT in scope (those are customer-specific and already pinned)
- a couple internal healthcheck CNAMEs used by probes

Miles Thornton: +1 to being careful. Remember TTL is only one lever; resolvers don’t always respect low TTL.

Emily Zhao: confirming: some enterprise customers terminate behind corporate resolvers that clamp to min TTL (I’ve seen 30s/60s). Also Java apps tend to cache DNS “forever” unless configured (security property). So lower TTL helps, but it’s not deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side: we’re trying to get faster convergence during a region impairment, but we don’t want surprise traffic shifts during normal ops. If TTL change causes more query load or weird caching behavior, that’s a bad trade.

Victor Alvarez: risks I’m tracking:
1) Resolver TTL floors (min TTL) => won’t see benefit for some clients
2) Negative caching (NXDOMAIN) if we misconfigure or a provider returns transient NXDOMAIN
3) Increased QPS to authoritative (Route53) and potential throttling (unlikely but worth quantifying)
4) Partial propagation mismatches during change window => weird split-brain (some clients re-resolve, others don’t)

Allison Grant: observability angle: if we do this, we should annotate dashboards and watch:
- DNS query rate at Route53
- `5xx` + tail latency by region
- ingress RPS shifts (esp. eu-west)
If there’s a subtle traffic drift we need to detect it fast.

Peter Holtz: also watch retries. Lower TTL can cause more reconnect churn on clients that resolve per request, but main risk is *failback* when a region recovers. If the record flips back quickly, some clients will thrash. We should pair with hold-down timers in routing policy.

Miles Thornton: right, DNS TTL != policy flap protection. The controller has hold-down, but DNS-based steering has to respect that too (don’t “bounce” answers).

Irene Choi: provider caveats:
- Some recursive resolvers serve stale records longer under load/outage (serve-stale RFC). During an internet event, low TTL might not matter.
- Cloudflare / public resolvers sometimes prefetch and extend caching behavior.
- On AWS, Route53 health checks can lag; but we’re not relying solely on R53 health checks for failover anyway.

Victor Alvarez: yep, this TTL change is meant to reduce the *best-case* convergence time when routing weights are adjusted, not to be the only trigger.

Emily Zhao: question: are we changing TTL for apex/ALIAS? If it’s ALIAS to a load balancer, TTL semantics are a bit weird. Route53 sets TTL but ALIAS answers can behave differently than plain A/AAAA from a client POV.

Victor Alvarez: `api.*` is ALIAS to regional NLBs via latency-based routing. TTL should still apply, but yes: some resolvers treat ALIAS just like A records. We’ll test with `dig` against common resolvers.

Rafael Mendes: can we do staged rollout? like: lower TTL only for a canary hostname first, run a planned weight flip in staging, then do prod.

Victor Alvarez: rollout plan draft:
Phase 0 (today): add canary record `api-canary.redwoodinference.com` with TTL=20s, same routing policy as `api.*`.
Phase 1 (staging): run a controlled weight shift us-east -> eu-west and measure convergence. Record:
- time-to-traffic-shift at edge (ingress)
- DNS query rates
- client error/latency
Phase 2 (prod, low blast): change TTL to 30s first on `stream.api.*` only (lower traffic), observe 24h
Phase 3 (prod): change TTL to 20s on `api.*` during a weekday window w/ oncall present
Rollback: revert TTL to 60s + stop further changes if:
- Route53 qps spikes >2x baseline for >15m
- unexplained region traffic shift >10% sustained

Sean Gallagher: I like staged. But I’d prefer 30s as final unless we can prove 20s doesn’t create noise. The bigger win might be getting rid of the 300s outliers.

Irene Choi: +1. Removing 300s is low-risk / high value.

Victor Alvarez: fair. Alternative proposal:
- Standardize to 60s everywhere
- Drop only failover steering records to 30s (not 20s)
- Keep canary at 20s for measurement

Miles Thornton: works for me. Also check: some mobile clients use OS caching with min TTL (iOS) + captive networks. Don’t assume perfect.

Allison Grant: if we do canary, please ensure dashboards include: `dns_ttl_seconds` label or annotation so we can correlate.

Victor Alvarez: we can add an annotation in the failover dashboard + a runbook note. I can also emit a one-time “infra change” event to the timeline feed.

Peter Holtz: any interaction with anycast? we have anycast notes in the networking doc but our public endpoint is still DNS->regional NLB. Anycast is only for internal edge proxies in a couple POPs, right?

Victor Alvarez: correct. No anycast change here.

Emily Zhao: another caveat: if we lower TTL but keep connection reuse high (HTTP/2), many clients won’t re-resolve until reconnect. So the effective traffic shift time is bounded by connection lifetimes too.

Miles Thornton: which is why the gateway redirect + retry-after stuff matters. DNS is just one piece.

- Victor: update tf PR to set TTL=30s for prod records, add canary at 20s
- Allison: prep dashboard annotation + quick query for Route53 QPS
- Miles/Victor: add note to game day plan re: DNS TTL expectations (not deterministic)

Irene Choi: can you also add a guardrail in the PR description: “no other routing policy changes included” to keep scope tight.

Victor Alvarez: yep, will do. Also adding a checklist:
- [ ] verify no 300s TTL remain on failover records
- [ ] verify healthcheck CNAMEs unchanged
- [ ] confirm Route53 limits + current qps baseline

Sean Gallagher: and include explicit “stop” conditions + who has rollback authority (infra oncall + SRE lead). We don’t want to debate mid-change.

Victor Alvarez: ack. Rollback authority: infra oncall (primary) with SRE lead consulted, Irene as final escalation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once PR is in, I’ll add the dashboard annotations & a temporary alert on Route53 qps anomalies.

Miles Thornton: one more thing: make sure we don’t reduce TTL on any records that are used for data residency pinning (some enterprise tenants depend on region-locked hostnames).

Victor Alvarez: confirmed out of scope. Only the global Hosted API hostnames.

Irene Choi: ok. +1 to proceed with the 30s plan + canary at 20s. Let’s schedule staging measurement before prod change.

Victor Alvarez: sounds good. I’ll update PR + drop a calendar hold for staging test with Emily + Allison.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.
text:
Victor Alvarez: heads up, we’re proposing DNS TTL reductions for regional failover (Hosted + some Dedicated endpoints). Want a quick risk assessment here before we merge the tf changes.

Victor Alvarez: current state (most public records): TTL=60s (a few are 300s still). Proposal: drop to 20s for the failover-weighted records, keep everything else at 60s.

Irene Choi: 20s is… aggressive but maybe fine. Can you clarify which records exactly? `api.redwoodinference.com` is behind the geo/latency policy right?

Victor Alvarez: yep. Specifically:
- `api.redwoodinference.com` (latency/weighted policy)
- `stream.api.redwoodinference.com`
- `dedicated.<region>.redwoodinference.com` NOT in scope (those are customer-specific and already pinned)
- a couple internal healthcheck CNAMEs used by probes

Miles Thornton: +1 to being careful. Remember TTL is only one lever; resolvers don’t always respect low TTL.

Emily Zhao: confirming: some enterprise customers terminate behind corporate resolvers that clamp to min TTL (I’ve seen 30s/60s). Also Java apps tend to cache DNS “forever” unless configured (security property). So lower TTL helps, but it’s not deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side: we’re trying to get faster convergence during a region impairment, but we don’t want surprise traffic shifts during normal ops. If TTL change causes more query load or weird caching behavior, that’s a bad trade.

Victor Alvarez: risks I’m tracking:
1) Resolver TTL floors (min TTL) => won’t see benefit for some clients
2) Negative caching (NXDOMAIN) if we misconfigure or a provider returns transient NXDOMAIN
3) Increased QPS to authoritative (Route53) and potential throttling (unlikely but worth quantifying)
4) Partial propagation mismatches during change window => weird split-brain (some clients re-resolve, others don’t)

Allison Grant: observability angle: if we do this, we should annotate dashboards and watch:
- DNS query rate at Route53
- `5xx` + tail latency by region
- ingress RPS shifts (esp. eu-west)
If there’s a subtle traffic drift we need to detect it fast.

Peter Holtz: also watch retries. Lower TTL can cause more reconnect churn on clients that resolve per request, but main risk is *failback* when a region recovers. If the record flips back quickly, some clients will thrash. We should pair with hold-down timers in routing policy.

Miles Thornton: right, DNS TTL != policy flap protection. The controller has hold-down, but DNS-based steering has to respect that too (don’t “bounce” answers).

Irene Choi: provider caveats:
- Some recursive resolvers serve stale records longer under load/outage (serve-stale RFC). During an internet event, low TTL might not matter.
- Cloudflare / public resolvers sometimes prefetch and extend caching behavior.
- On AWS, Route53 health checks can lag; but we’re not relying solely on R53 health checks for failover anyway.

Victor Alvarez: yep, this TTL change is meant to reduce the *best-case* convergence time when routing weights are adjusted, not to be the only trigger.

Emily Zhao: question: are we changing TTL for apex/ALIAS? If it’s ALIAS to a load balancer, TTL semantics are a bit weird. Route53 sets TTL but ALIAS answers can behave differently than plain A/AAAA from a client POV.

Victor Alvarez: `api.*` is ALIAS to regional NLBs via latency-based routing. TTL should still apply, but yes: some resolvers treat ALIAS just like A records. We’ll test with `dig` against common resolvers.

Rafael Mendes: can we do staged rollout? like: lower TTL only for a canary hostname first, run a planned weight flip in staging, then do prod.

Victor Alvarez: rollout plan draft:
Phase 0 (today): add canary record `api-canary.redwoodinference.com` with TTL=20s, same routing policy as `api.*`.
Phase 1 (staging): run a controlled weight shift us-east -> eu-west and measure convergence. Record:
- time-to-traffic-shift at edge (ingress)
- DNS query rates
- client error/latency
Phase 2 (prod, low blast): change TTL to 30s first on `stream.api.*` only (lower traffic), observe 24h
Phase 3 (prod): change TTL to 20s on `api.*` during a weekday window w/ oncall present
Rollback: revert TTL to 60s + stop further changes if:
- Route53 qps spikes >2x baseline for >15m
- unexplained region traffic shift >10% sustained

Sean Gallagher: I like staged. But I’d prefer 30s as final unless we can prove 20s doesn’t create noise. The bigger win might be getting rid of the 300s outliers.

Irene Choi: +1. Removing 300s is low-risk / high value.

Victor Alvarez: fair. Alternative proposal:
- Standardize to 60s everywhere
- Drop only failover steering records to 30s (not 20s)
- Keep canary at 20s for measurement

Miles Thornton: works for me. Also check: some mobile clients use OS caching with min TTL (iOS) + captive networks. Don’t assume perfect.

Allison Grant: if we do canary, please ensure dashboards include: `dns_ttl_seconds` label or annotation so we can correlate.

Victor Alvarez: we can add an annotation in the failover dashboard + a runbook note. I can also emit a one-time “infra change” event to the timeline feed.

Peter Holtz: any interaction with anycast? we have anycast notes in the networking doc but our public endpoint is still DNS->regional NLB. Anycast is only for internal edge proxies in a couple POPs, right?

Victor Alvarez: correct. No anycast change here.

Emily Zhao: another caveat: if we lower TTL but keep connection reuse high (HTTP/2), many clients won’t re-resolve until reconnect. So the effective traffic shift time is bounded by connection lifetimes too.

Miles Thornton: which is why the gateway redirect + retry-after stuff matters. DNS is just one piece.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Victor: update tf PR to set TTL=30s for prod records, add canary at 20s
- Allison: prep dashboard annotation + quick query for Route53 QPS
- Miles/Victor: add note to game day plan re: DNS TTL expectations (not deterministic)

Terraform Bot: PR opened: redwood-terraform/pr-1442-dns-ttl-and-failover-routing-updates (diff includes TTL changes for `api.redwoodinference.com` + new `api-canary.redwoodinference.com`).

Irene Choi: can you also add a guardrail in the PR description: “no other routing policy changes included” to keep scope tight.

Victor Alvarez: yep, will do. Also adding a checklist:
- [ ] verify no 300s TTL remain on failover records
- [ ] verify healthcheck CNAMEs unchanged
- [ ] confirm Route53 limits + current qps baseline

Sean Gallagher: and include explicit “stop” conditions + who has rollback authority (infra oncall + SRE lead). We don’t want to debate mid-change.

Victor Alvarez: ack. Rollback authority: infra oncall (primary) with SRE lead consulted, Irene as final escalation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once PR is in, I’ll add the dashboard annotations & a temporary alert on Route53 qps anomalies.

Miles Thornton: one more thing: make sure we don’t reduce TTL on any records that are used for data residency pinning (some enterprise tenants depend on region-locked hostnames).

Victor Alvarez: confirmed out of scope. Only the global Hosted API hostnames.

Irene Choi: ok. +1 to proceed with the 30s plan + canary at 20s. Let’s schedule staging measurement before prod change.

Victor Alvarez: sounds good. I’ll update PR + drop a calendar hold for staging test with Emily + Allison.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.

title: #eng-oncall: 1767660044
preview:
Rafael Mendes: putting together oncall procedures for KV compaction canary. need crisp guidance: when to flip kill-switch vs just reduce budgets, what dashboards to look at, and who to pull in. pls drop thoughts here.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should make it very binary for oncall: “is compaction likely the cause” -> “do X”. last canary we spent 20 min debating.

Allison Grant: dashboards first. for compaction specifically we should require checking:
- Hosted API Tail Latency (p95/p99) by route/model
- KV allocator health: fragmentation_ratio, free_list_depth, largest_contig_block_bytes
- compaction: bytes_moved_total, compaction_step_time_ms, compaction_budget_hit_count
- eviction: kv_evictions_total (by reason + tier), pinned_eviction_failures
- GPU: mem_used, allocator_oom_count, request_shed_count

Rafael Mendes: do we have canonical links yet or should I just put the grafana search keywords?

Allison Grant: links are fine if stable. otherwise “search: kv.fragmentation_ratio” etc. (and include metric names exactly)

Benji Okafor: kill-switch knobs we have in runtime config (today):
- runtime.kv.compaction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick
- runtime.kv.compaction.max_bytes_per_tick
- runtime.kv.eviction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min
plus a “safe mode” we discussed: set budget to 0 instead of full disable (lets compaction state machine drain without moving pages). not sure if we landed it.

Samir Qureshi: safe mode exists in latest canary build: if enabled=false, relocation stops immediately but leaves any in-flight relocation to finish current step (we guard with refcounts). if budget_ms=0 but enabled=true, it keeps measuring fragmentation + will schedule zero move work (useful to confirm correlation w/o fully toggling).

Sean Gallagher: oncall guidance should be:
1) first response: reduce compaction budget to 0 (NOT full disable) if p99 spikes and compaction_step_time_ms correlates.
2) if p99 still bad, or we see allocator_oom_count climbing, disable compaction + disable eviction (eviction can also cause churn/extra copies)
3) if OOM / shedding: disable compaction immediately, then page runtime.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: in OOM scenario you might want eviction ON (to prevent hard OOM) but compaction OFF (to reduce copy overhead). depends on what’s triggering. if the OOM is from fragmentation, eviction might *help* while we back off compaction.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “when to keep eviction on” as a rule?

Omar El-Sayed: suggestion:
- If we see rising allocator_oom_count OR request_shed_count due to KV allocation failures -> keep eviction enabled, cap evictions_per_min to avoid thrash.
- If we see quality issues / session resets / elevated “sequence_aborted” (not sure metric name) -> consider eviction off, and we may need runtime to look.

Allison Grant: metrics we have for eviction impact:
- kv.eviction.abort_sequence_total
- kv.eviction.pinned_skip_total
- kv.eviction.prefix_cache_drop_total
not perfect but gives signal.

Sean Gallagher: threshold triggers: can we put numbers? e.g.
- if p99 decode latency +20% for >10m in canary cohort AND compaction bytes moved > baseline by 2x
- or compaction_step_time_ms > 5ms p95 -> action

Paula Mendes: rollout automation is already watching p99 + error rate, but oncall doc should say what triggers manual rollback/disable:
- p99 > 1.25x baseline for 10m (canary)
- request_shed_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- allocator_oom_count > 0 sustained
If any of those + compaction metrics are “active”, kill-switch is authorized.

Mateo Alvarez: authority: SRE/eng-oncall can flip kill-switch in an incident without runtime approval. just notify #eng-runtime and open incident channel. if not an incident, please loop Omar/Samir first.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll put that explicitly: “if paging incident, flip first, ask questions after.”

Benji Okafor: config propagation latency: expect 30-90s to hit all hosts in a pool; dedicated can be slower if control plane is busy. oncall should watch the “config applied” metric (we emit runtime.config.last_applied_ts).

Samir Qureshi: also note: disabling compaction doesn’t reclaim already-fragmented layout immediately. improvement should be “no further spikes from moving pages”. fragmentation_ratio may stay high until churn evens out.

Sean Gallagher: escalation path draft:
- If p99 spike + compaction correlated: page Runtime KV (Omar), then Allocator (Samir)
- If correctness suspicion (bad outputs / crashes in relocation): page Noah Patel
- If GPU copy kernel hot / high SM util during compaction: page Grace Kim (kernels)
- For rollback help / flag propagation issues: page Benji

Rafael Mendes: yes. also add “SRE primary owns incident + comms; runtime owns mitigation + followup PR.”

Allison Grant: add a “what dashboards to screenshot” section for incident notes:
- p99 chart with cohort filter
- fragmentation_ratio + bytes_moved overlay
- eviction rate
- allocator_oom_count + shed

Sean Gallagher: command snippets? makes it easier.

Benji Okafor: examples (internal):
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick --value 0`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.enabled --value false`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min --value 60`
(+ include “verify” command: `rwdctl config get ...`)

Omar El-Sayed: also add “verify in runtime logs”: look for `kv_compaction state=disabled reason=operator` and `compaction_budget_ms=0` lines. ppl trust logs more than dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. I’ll draft runbook section:
- Triage checklist (5 min)
- Decision tree for budget=0 vs disable vs eviction changes
- Verification steps (dashboard + logs + config applied timestamp)
- Escalations + who can flip

Incident Bot: Reminder: If this is customer-impacting, open an incident in #incidents and attach dashboards + config change log.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: if we flip kill-switch, add a follow-up task to re-enable w/ smaller budget + canary only. don’t leave it off forever.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll put a “post-mitigation” checklist: (1) file retro note, (2) revert to canary defaults next business day w/ runtime owner, (3) update thresholds if alerts were noisy.

title: #eng-sre: 1763611907
preview:
Rafael Mendes: post-launch metrics check for hot-route protection (GA enablement window ended ~2h ago). Can folks sanity check: tail latency + 5xx for protected routes vs last week? any weirdness w/ shed/429 reason codes?

Sergio Costa: looking at the "Hot Route Protection - Enterprise" dashboard (us-east + eu-west). Early read is good.
- /v1/chat/completions enterprise p99: 3.9s -> 2.6s (24h rolling)
- /v1/embeddings enterprise p99: 1.3s -> 0.9s
- 5xx rate: down ~45% on protected routes (mostly timeouts)
Annotations line up w/ flag enablement.

Aisha Bello: +1. Error budget burn improved too.
- Chat enterprise SLO (99.9%): burn rate was ~2.1x during peaks last week; now hovering ~0.7x since rollout.
- Best-effort getting throttled earlier (expected), but seeing fewer fleet-wide latency spikes.
I’m not seeing any paging storms from the new alerts either (nice).

incident-bot: Overload protection activations (last 60m):
- region=us-east route_group=chat tier=best_effort action=admission_control events=7 max_shed_rate=18% duration_max=6m dashboard=https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/hot-route-protect
- region=eu-west route_group=embeddings tier=best_effort action=quota_gate events=2 max_429_rps=220 duration_max=2m
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook+Overload+Protection+and+Admission+Control

Logan Wright: telemetry view: overload_reason_code breakdown looks clean (no new high-cardinality offenders).
Top 429 reasons since GA:
- best-effort: OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (62%), QUOTA_EXCEEDED (34%), POLICY_DENY (4%)
- enterprise: QUOTA_EXCEEDED (88%), OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (8%), ROUTE_BUDGET (4%)
So enterprise is mostly quota, not overload shedding.

Rafael Mendes: love to see enterprise not getting punted. For best-effort 18% shed rate spikes — does that correlate w/ GPU saturation?

Vanessa Ortiz: checked fleet signals (us-east). Those 6m windows correspond to GPU util ~92-95% + queue depth crossing the trigger threshold. Autoscaler was already adding nodes but warmup lag meant the routing gate kicked in first.
Net effect: enterprise queue depth stays flat, best-effort queue depth stops growing (no cascade).

Ethan Park: gateway side looks stable. No increase in gateway CPU, policy eval cache hit rate ~97%.
We did see a small uptick in 429s overall (+12% vs baseline) but that’s concentrated in best-effort and matches the new behavior.

Aisha Bello: any reports from Support yet on “why am I getting more 429s”? I expect some, but want to make sure messaging points to retry/backoff + reason code headers.

Logan Wright: headers are present in samples I pulled:
`x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_admission`
`retry-after: 1`
Also seeing the structured body in most clients. There are a few missing `retry-after` (looks like old edge pods?) — might be a rollout straggler.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a panel to track “429 missing retry-after header” rate. If it’s >0.5% we should chase it.

Rafael Mendes: action items / follow-ups:
1) Create ticket to investigate missing `retry-after` on some 429s (assign gateway). We should not make Support guess.
2) Tune eu-west embeddings threshold slightly? We’re seeing quota_gate events; might be config mismatch or just traffic burst.
3) Add a short internal note for on-call: what shed % is “expected” before we page (so folks don’t panic).

Ethan Park: I’ll open (1) as ENG follow-up. Suspect some pods still on legacy throttle middleware. We can confirm by build hash.

Aisha Bello: for (3), I can PR the runbook w/ a “normal vs abnormal” section. E.g. best-effort shed up to 20% for <10m w/ stable enterprise p99 is OK; page if enterprise shed >1% sustained 5m or enterprise p99 burn >2x for 10m.

Vanessa Ortiz: on (2) — eu-west had a capacity dip earlier (one GPU pool drained for maintenance). If we’re stable now, I’d prefer we don’t loosen thresholds until we have a week of data. Maybe we just add an annotation for that maintenance window.

Sergio Costa: +1. Also seeing enterprise p95 stable in eu-west, so protections did what we wanted.

Rafael Mendes: cool. Can someone link the post-launch review ticket? want everything captured.

Aisha Bello: ENG-4931 is the post-launch metrics review + followups. I’ll comment with today’s deltas + these actions.

Logan Wright: I’ll also drop a note re: cardinality staying flat + the minor `retry-after` gap.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. If no fires by EOD, we can call this “healthy” and shift to the cleanup items. :thumbsup:

- region=eu-west route_group=embeddings tier=best_effort action=quota_gate events=2 max_429_rps=220 duration_max=2m
Runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Runbook+Overload+Protection+and+Admission+Control

Logan Wright: telemetry view: overload_reason_code breakdown looks clean (no new high-cardinality offenders).
Top 429 reasons since GA:
- best-effort: OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (62%), QUOTA_EXCEEDED (34%), POLICY_DENY (4%)
- enterprise: QUOTA_EXCEEDED (88%), OVERLOAD_ADMISSION (8%), ROUTE_BUDGET (4%)
So enterprise is mostly quota, not overload shedding.

Rafael Mendes: love to see enterprise not getting punted. For best-effort 18% shed rate spikes — does that correlate w/ GPU saturation?

Vanessa Ortiz: checked fleet signals (us-east). Those 6m windows correspond to GPU util ~92-95% + queue depth crossing the trigger threshold. Autoscaler was already adding nodes but warmup lag meant the routing gate kicked in first.
Net effect: enterprise queue depth stays flat, best-effort queue depth stops growing (no cascade).

Ethan Park: gateway side looks stable. No increase in gateway CPU, policy eval cache hit rate ~97%.
We did see a small uptick in 429s overall (+12% vs baseline) but that’s concentrated in best-effort and matches the new behavior.

Aisha Bello: any reports from Support yet on “why am I getting more 429s”? I expect some, but want to make sure messaging points to retry/backoff + reason code headers.

Logan Wright: headers are present in samples I pulled:
`x-redwood-throttle-reason: overload_admission`
`retry-after: 1`
Also seeing the structured body in most clients. There are a few missing `retry-after` (looks like old edge pods?) — might be a rollout straggler.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a panel to track “429 missing retry-after header” rate. If it’s >0.5% we should chase it.

Rafael Mendes: action items / follow-ups:
1) Create ticket to investigate missing `retry-after` on some 429s (assign gateway). We should not make Support guess.
2) Tune eu-west embeddings threshold slightly? We’re seeing quota_gate events; might be config mismatch or just traffic burst.
3) Add a short internal note for on-call: what shed % is “expected” before we page (so folks don’t panic).

Ethan Park: I’ll open (1) as ENG follow-up. Suspect some pods still on legacy throttle middleware. We can confirm by build hash.

Aisha Bello: for (3), I can PR the runbook w/ a “normal vs abnormal” section. E.g. best-effort shed up to 20% for <10m w/ stable enterprise p99 is OK; page if enterprise shed >1% sustained 5m or enterprise p99 burn >2x for 10m.

Vanessa Ortiz: on (2) — eu-west had a capacity dip earlier (one GPU pool drained for maintenance). If we’re stable now, I’d prefer we don’t loosen thresholds until we have a week of data. Maybe we just add an annotation for that maintenance window.

Sergio Costa: +1. Also seeing enterprise p95 stable in eu-west, so protections did what we wanted.

Rafael Mendes: cool. Can someone link the post-launch review ticket? want everything captured.

Aisha Bello: ENG-4931 is the post-launch metrics review + followups. I’ll comment with today’s deltas + these actions.

Logan Wright: I’ll also drop a note re: cardinality staying flat + the minor `retry-after` gap.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. If no fires by EOD, we can call this “healthy” and shift to the cleanup items. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-infra: 1762643912
preview:
Irene Choi: heads up, for hot-route protection we need to settle which fleet signals are actually reliable for admission control triggers. seeing debate in reviews: GPU saturation vs queue depth vs scheduler lag. want to lock a recommendation this week + note region-specific thresholds.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. GPU % is the one everyone reaches for but it lies a lot in practice (esp in us-east). queue depth seems closer to “customer pain” but also depends on batching settings.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE view: the main thing is signals that correlate with p95/p99 latency BEFORE we’re on fire. GPU util tends to peg at 95% during healthy steady state for some pools.

Keiko Yamamoto: also autoscaler can mask things. gpu-util can be high while we’re scaling out, so if we shed based on util we’d shed too aggressively.

Jamal Pierce: i pulled a quick snapshot from last week burst (us-east) — GPU util was 92–97% for ~40 mins, but latency only spiked for chat when queue depth crossed a threshold.

Jamal Pierce: excerpt (from notes):
- us-east chat pool baseline gpu_util: ~88–93% at normal daytime
- eu-west baseline gpu_util: ~70–82%
- queue_depth_req (avg) baseline: ~0.2–0.8
- when queue_depth_req sustained > 6, p95 latency started climbing fast

Robert King: so maybe gpu util isn’t a trigger, but still useful as context / to validate we’re not just in a metric bug.

Elena Popov: routing needs something stable and low-cardinality. per-GPU metrics are expensive in decision path; aggregated per-pool / per-cluster is fine. queue depth per route-group would be ideal.

Julian Moore: runtime side: scheduler lag is probably the cleanest leading indicator. We now export `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` + `runtime.queue_depth_requests` (post PR-27435). Both are already aggregated by pool.

Logan Wright: careful on tags. we had the cardinality incident last month when route_id slipped into overload metrics :grimacing: if routing wants per-route-group, we can do `route_group` not per-tenant.

Aisha Bello: +1. The alerting side also prefers a composite: (queue depth high) AND (sched lag high) for N minutes.

Irene Choi: what about GPU saturation as a guardrail though? e.g. if queue depth low but GPU is pegged, that’s still a sign we’re out of headroom? or could just be long sequences / kernels.

Sofia Ivanova: queue depth low + gpu pegged is often long prompts with continuous batching. scheduler is “busy” but not necessarily behind. it only becomes a problem if lag grows (scheduler can’t keep up) or queue accumulates.

Vanessa Ortiz: also MIG fragmentation can make util look high with less effective throughput. we’ve seen util ~95% even when one slice is starved.

Peter Holtz: for circuit breakers we usually look at:
1) scheduler lag p95 (service time backlog)
2) pending queue depth (arrival > service)
3) 5xx spike (already failing)
GPU util is mostly a “capacity planning” indicator not “admission now” indicator.

Robert King: question: do we have consistent queue depth across regions? i remember eu-west uses a slightly different batching config.

Julian Moore: yes but magnitude differs. queue depth is “requests waiting to be admitted to scheduler” so it depends on front-door concurrency + batch size.

Jamal Pierce: that’s where region-specific thresholds come in. eu-west queue depth baseline is lower; using a global threshold of 6 might be too high (we’d react late). maybe use percentile over baseline per region/pool.

Aisha Bello: +100. absolute thresholds are brittle. can we do: trigger when `queue_depth` > max(absolute_min, baseline_p95 * factor)?

Keiko Yamamoto: baseline computation in routing sounds risky for data plane complexity. but we can precompute per-region thresholds in config (control plane) and ship them.

Elena Popov: yep, we can keep routing simple:
- read per-route-group budget
- read per-region “pressure thresholds” for pool
- evaluate with last N seconds aggregated metrics from fleet signals service
No dynamic baseline calc in routing.

Irene Choi: can infra own the per-region threshold table (at least initial values) and we can revisit after canary?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. propose:
Signal A (primary): scheduler_lag_ms_p95
Signal B (secondary): queue_depth_requests_avg (or p95)
Signal C (context only): gpu_util_p95

Vanessa Ortiz: then define pressure states per region/pool:
- WARN: lag > X OR qdepth > Y for 2m
- HOT: lag > X2 AND qdepth > Y2 for 1m
- CRIT: lag > X3 AND qdepth > Y3 for 30s (activate strict admission)

Rafael Mendes: this maps nicely to “start shedding best-effort at HOT, protect enterprise budgets at CRIT”.

Logan Wright: for dashboards, please standardize names:
- `fleet.pressure_state{region,pool}` as a gauge (0/1/2/3)
- `overload.shed_rate{region,route_group,tier,reason}`
We can annotate when pressure_state flips.

Peter Holtz: note: pressure_state should be derived in control plane / fleet signals service, not computed differently by each component.

Robert King: do we have an agreed metric source? i’ve seen both prometheus and the fleet-signals gRPC.

Vanessa Ortiz: for this project: fleet-signals gRPC (aggregated) is the source of truth. prom is for debugging.

Jamal Pierce: region notes (tentative, based on last 14d):
- us-east (chat pool): WARN lag>120ms or qdepth>4; HOT lag>250ms + qdepth>8; CRIT lag>400ms + qdepth>12
- eu-west (chat pool): WARN lag>90ms or qdepth>3; HOT lag>200ms + qdepth>6; CRIT lag>320ms + qdepth>9
These need validation during game day.

Aisha Bello: add a “don’t flap” rule please. if we enter HOT/CRIT require 60s below WARN to exit.

Keiko Yamamoto: also check autoscaling interaction: when scale-out in progress, lag can oscillate. maybe incorporate `pending_scale_actions` as a dampener? (not a trigger)

Sofia Ivanova: be careful: when scale-out happens, new replicas warm KV cache etc. you still might want to shed briefly to protect tail.

Irene Choi: ok, decision direction:
- use scheduler lag + queue depth for triggers
- GPU util is contextual only
- thresholds per region/pool in config, owned by infra + tuned via canary/game day

Irene Choi: action items:
1) @Jamal Pierce share the threshold table draft in the rollout doc + include “how derived”
2) @Vanessa Ortiz + @Robert King confirm fleet-signals gRPC fields + update contract doc
3) @Elena Popov ensure routing consumes pressure_state + doesn’t query raw gpu util
4) @Logan Wright align dashboard queries + make sure tags are bounded (region/pool/route_group only)

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll post the gRPC fields here once i confirm final names.

Julian Moore: current names from runtime exporter (double-check):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95{region,pool}`
- `runtime.queue_depth_requests_avg{region,pool}`
- `runtime.gpu_utilization_p95{region,pool}`
Fleet-signals service already aggregates to `fleet.*` equivalents.

Rafael Mendes: once we have the table, we’ll run it in the game day and see if we start shedding at the right time (before enterprise p95 blows past SLO). :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: cool. please keep this thread updated with any “gotchas” discovered in eu-west; we’ve been burned by copying us-east thresholds before.

title: #eng-oncall: 1762400113
preview:
Peter Holtz: quick q for whoever’s around — seeing a bunch of new 429s in us-east canary. what are the *official* reason codes now, and which runbook section do we follow for overload vs quota? trying not to thrash.

incident-bot: (FYI) hot-route protection active: route_group=chat_completions tier=best_effort action=admission_control shed_rate=0.18 region=us-east

Owen Phillips: +1, Support pinging too. The response headers now include `x-redwood-429-reason` but I don’t remember the mapping.

Amara Diallo: hey — gateway side: we standardized on `x-redwood-429-reason` + consistent JSON error.

Amara Diallo: current values we’re emitting:
- `quota_exceeded` (hard quota window exhausted)
- `rate_limited` (per-second/minute limit hit; usually client burst)
- `overload_admission_control` (capacity protection kicking in; best-effort gets gated first)
- `overload_shed` (we accepted but then shed due to sustained pressure / queue depth)

Amara Diallo: also check `retry-after` (seconds) when present. we also set `x-redwood-retry-after-ms` for SDKs.

Peter Holtz: perfect. what’s the runbook split? overload vs quota path?

Rafael Mendes: overload path == anything starting with `overload_*`.

Rafael Mendes: Runbook is: "Runbook: overload protection and admission control".
- If `overload_admission_control`: start at section "Admission control active (best-effort gating)" (verify enterprise SLO stable, then confirm signals: queue_depth + scheduler_lag + gpu_saturation)
- If `overload_shed`: go to "Load shedding / request shedding" section (look at shed reason breakdown + whether a config change triggered it)

Rafael Mendes: quota path == `quota_exceeded` or `rate_limited`:
- `quota_exceeded`: follow "Quota evaluation" section + confirm tenant window counters (and whether they’re on the right tier)
- `rate_limited`: usually expected client-side; we just verify limit config and that it’s not accidentally applied to enterprise route_group

Aisha Bello: + for alerting/paging: we *don’t* page on best-effort `overload_admission_control` unless enterprise p95/p99 or 5xx burn is impacted. Pager should be driven by enterprise SLO burn alerts / protected routes.

Owen Phillips: is there a quick rule for “is this expected during pressure” vs “something broken”?

Rafael Mendes: heuristics:
- expected: best-effort 429s increase with `x-redwood-429-reason=overload_admission_control`, enterprise dashboards flat, no 5xx spike
- suspicious: enterprise tier getting `overload_*`, or 429 reason is `overload_shed` with rising 5xx + scheduler_lag climbing fast (possible cascade)

Amara Diallo: also FYI the body looks like:
`{"error":{"type":"rate_limit","code":"429","message":"Request throttled","reason":"overload_admission_control"}}`
(“reason” should match header)

Peter Holtz: amazing. can you drop the exact confluence link/section anchor? I’m on mobile.

Aisha Bello: confluence path: /eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-overload-protection-and-admission-control
anchors are roughly:
- #Admission-control-active
- #Load-shedding
- #Quota-vs-overload-quick-triage

Owen Phillips: :thumbsup: I’ll reply to Support with the mapping + “overload_* => overload runbook”

Peter Holtz: thanks all. closing loop on oncall notes — for current event, it’s best-effort gating only. will monitor enterprise burn + shed rate panels.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: if enterprise sees `overload_admission_control` at all, page SRE immediately + roll back allowlist / flag per runbook "Rollback criteria".

title: #architecture: 1762231191
preview:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers
- (Allison/Rafael) clarify metrics vs logs + dashboard annotations

Ravi Menon: will repost revised draft tomorrow AM.
text:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers

title: #eng-oncall: 1760464200
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Comms Lead training Q&A thread. Drop questions here as you go through the deck/runbook. Biggest topic last session: what to do when root cause is unknown + we’re actively mitigating.

Christine Wong: 🙋‍♀️ When we’re in that awkward phase: dashboards red, we’re traffic shifting / rolling back, but we don’t know RC yet… do we post “Mitigating” or stay “Investigating” until we’re confident?

Wei Chen: +1. Also what do we *say* about mitigations without speculating / leaking details?

Rafael Mendes: Good question. Default guidance:
- Use **Investigating** until you can state a *fact* about what’s happening (symptom) and what we’re doing (action), without guessing cause.
- You can move to **Mitigating** once we’ve started a concrete mitigation and have evidence it’s helping OR it’s the primary active workstream.
- You do **not** need root cause to say “we’re applying mitigations.”

Miles Thornton: the thing we’re optimizing for is clarity + tempo, not perfect certainty. “Mitigating” can basically mean “we’re taking steps to reduce impact” even if RC unknown.

Christine Wong: ok, but what’s the line between “action” and “speculation”? e.g. we flipped fallback, or shifted traffic to another region.

Wei Chen: and do we mention “failover” or “capacity” publicly? sometimes those are sensitive.

Kira Thompson: on sensitivity: avoid anything that implies customer-specific capacity constraints or “we ran out of GPUs.” Better to frame as “traffic is being rerouted / we’re scaling capacity” without details.

Dev Patel: from Support side, customers mainly want: what’s broken, where, what’s the workaround, and when they’ll hear next. They do NOT need kernel-level explanations.

Rafael Mendes: yep. A good pattern when RC unknown:
1) symptom + component/region
2) mitigation *in progress* (what we’re doing, high-level)
3) workaround (if safe)
4) next update ETA

Allison Grant: also keep it consistent with signals. If it’s latency-only and errors are fine, don’t say “outage.” Say “degraded performance / elevated latency.”

Owen Phillips: can we get example copy blocks? it’s easier to follow with concrete text.

Rafael Mendes: example “Investigating” when RC unknown:
“Investigating: We’re investigating elevated error rates impacting **Hosted API → Streaming (us-east)**. We’ve engaged on-call engineers and are working to mitigate. Next update within 30 minutes or sooner.”

Rafael Mendes: example “Mitigating” without RC:
“Mitigating: We’ve identified a potential mitigation path and are actively applying changes to reduce impact to **Hosted API → Streaming (us-east)**. Some users may continue to see increased disconnects while changes roll out. Next update within 30 minutes or sooner.”

Christine Wong: that “potential mitigation path” line is nice. doesn’t overpromise.

Wei Chen: Should we ever say “we believe” / “likely” in public updates?

Rafael Mendes: sparingly. “We believe” is ok if it’s tied to an observable symptom (e.g., “we believe this is related to elevated upstream timeouts”) but avoid “likely hardware issue” / “probably provider” unless confirmed and ok’d.

Kira Thompson: + avoid naming vendors/providers in public comms unless Legal/Security has pre-approved wording.

Devon Price: incident-bot will help here. The draft templates intentionally avoid causal language and keep the mitigation text high-level. (PR-19344)

Incident-bot: (bot) Reminder: Comms Lead target TTFU is 5 minutes for Sev0/Sev1. Use the Investigating template if details are still emerging.

Dev Patel: If you can include a workaround, even minimal, it reduces tickets a lot. For streaming: “retry” / “temporarily use non-stream responses” is helpful if accurate.

Christine Wong: what if we don’t know a workaround yet? i don’t want to suggest “retry” if it makes things worse.

Rafael Mendes: then don’t. Don’t invent workarounds. You can say “We’ll share workarounds as we confirm them.”

Allison Grant: and if retries increase load, better to say “Some requests may fail; please use exponential backoff” only if SRE/IC agrees.

Wei Chen: approval flow question: if we’re posting Mitigating without RC, does it need Support approval?

Rafael Mendes: no approval needed for first Investigating. For Sev0/Sev1:
- Investigating: publish immediately.
- Identified/Mitigating: no formal approval required, but sanity check in #incidents if there’s time.
- Resolved: IC + Comms Lead must agree it’s actually stable; loop Support lead if there’s any ambiguity.

Owen Phillips: what about when the mitigation is “rollback” but we’re unsure it’ll work. ok to say rollback?

Rafael Mendes: yes, at a high level: “We’re rolling back a recent change” is fine. Avoid IDs/versions unless already public. Don’t say “bad deploy” unless confirmed.

Christine Wong: and when we’re doing traffic shifting across regions? customers interpret that as “region down.”

Kira Thompson: you can say “We’re rerouting traffic to reduce impact” but keep component/region status aligned to *experienced impact*. If us-east users are impacted, mark us-east degraded even if mitigation is routing elsewhere.

Allison Grant: + keep the component scope tight. If it’s only Streaming, don’t mark the whole Hosted API component.

Wei Chen: question on wording: “partial outage” vs “degraded performance” when errors are spiky but not total.

Rafael Mendes: use the thresholds guidance (burn-rate / sustained error %). If it’s intermittent elevated errors and customers are failing requests, “Degraded Performance” is usually right; “Partial Outage” when a meaningful subset consistently cannot use the feature.

Dev Patel: Support will ask: “is it safe to tell customers it’s partial outage?” We’d rather you underclaim early and correct upward than overclaim and backtrack.

Christine Wong: if root cause unknown but we’ve stabilized, do we go to Monitoring? what do we say?

Rafael Mendes: yes. “Monitoring” is for “mitigation applied, impact trending down, watching to ensure stability.” Example:
“Monitoring: Mitigations have been applied and error rates are returning to normal for **Hosted API → Streaming (us-east)**. We’re monitoring closely to ensure continued recovery. Next update within 60 minutes or sooner.”

Wei Chen: cool. also: please confirm we should always include next update time.

Rafael Mendes: yes. It’s part of consistency + reduces refresh anxiety.

Devon Price: fwiw we’re adding a bot reminder if an incident hasn’t had a public update in 30 mins during active phases.

Incident-bot: (bot) Tip: You can paste `INVESTIGATING_MIN` / `MITIGATING_MIN` blocks from the templates doc. Draft automation is rolling out in incident-bot next sprint.

Owen Phillips: dedicated/private question: if it’s a subset of Dedicated clusters, do we post to public status?

Kira Thompson: depends. General rule: public status page can reflect “Dedicated” at a high level when there’s broad impact, but avoid customer-identifying details. For single-tenant isolated issues, we usually handle via Support/CS direct comms + post-incident summary if policy requires.

Dev Patel: and if a dedicated customer is escalating, route through the escalation playbook. Support/CS owns direct customer updates; Comms Lead owns status page.

Christine Wong: this helps a lot. can we pin these example blocks somewhere?

Rafael Mendes: yep — I’ll add the exact examples to the Comms Lead section in the runbook + templates page and link back here. Keep questions coming as you run into edge cases.
