title: #architecture: 1759953233
preview:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.
text:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

title: #eng: 1759942015
preview:
Jae Kim: quick q on the status page component mapping work — for route/capability tagging, what’s the expected labels/values? i’m looking at gateway metrics + a couple workers and want to make sure we tag in the “approved” way so it actually maps to public components.

Logan Wright: yup, we standardized on 3 dims for anything that can drive status/public summaries:
- `component_id`
- `region`
- `capability`
This is the same set incident-bot is collecting in intake now.

Jae Kim: ok but what counts as “capability” vs “route”? e.g. `/v1/chat/completions` + stream=true is that “chat” or “streaming”? + do we include model family anywhere?

Mina Haddad: capability is intentionally coarse + user-visible. don’t put model names in tags.
Rules of thumb:
- `/v1/chat/completions` (non-stream) => `capability=chat`
- `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true` OR SSE endpoint => `capability=streaming`
- `/v1/embeddings` => `capability=embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank` => `capability=reranking`
If something is “internal route-ish” (scheduler calls, prefill service), keep that as internal labels but still map outward to a capability.

Christine Wong: +1. also: `component_id` is product surface, not service. for almost all request-path metrics in prod it should be `component_id=hosted_api` (Dedicated/Private are different; we can go over those separately).

Allison Grant: docs pointer: Confluence has the taxonomy + definitions: “Status page component taxonomy” (SRE → status-page-and-comms). the canonical ids are lowercase snake_case. pls don’t invent new ones ad-hoc.

Jae Kim: do we have a schema somewhere i can validate against? i keep fat-fingering region names.

Logan Wright: yep, schema is in the registry validation PR. the “single source of truth” is a JSON registry file (and a JSON schema that CI validates). you can search in the observability-pack repo for “status component registry schema”.

Caleb Johnson: regions should match what routing uses today (no cloud provider AZs). examples we’re using:
- `us-east`
- `us-west`
- `eu-west`
- `ap-southeast`
If you only know “global” (rare), we’d rather you plumb region through than tag `global`.

Devon Price: for incident-bot intake fields we also use those exact strings. so if you tag metrics with `region=us-east` and pick `capability=streaming`, the bot drafts will line up with the component options.

Jae Kim: can someone drop an example tag block for gateway?

Christine Wong: from the gateway side (pseudo):
```python
metrics.increment(
  "gateway.request",
  tags={
    "component_id": "hosted_api",
    "region": request.region,  # e.g. "us-east"
    "capability": capability_from_request(request),
  },
)
```
`capability_from_request` should be based on endpoint + stream flag. not on model.

Mina Haddad: and if you’re doing this in traces too, same keys. we want to be able to pivot dashboards + summaries by the same dims.

Allison Grant: re: “route” — if you really need it for debugging, keep it as `route_name` or `endpoint` internally, but do not use it as a public-facing grouping. we had the “API ok but embeddings failing” issue because things were tagged as generic API without the capability split.

Jae Kim: makes sense. what about dedicated control plane? i’m touching a metric in the capacity scheduler.

Rafael Mendes: dedicated control plane is a separate component_id (not `hosted_api`). the taxonomy doc calls it out as “Dedicated: Control Plane” and we’re exposing a limited set of signals publicly. if your metric affects customer-facing provisioning/scale actions, tag it to that. if it’s purely internal noise, don’t.

Devon Price: + note: dedicated/private incidents have comms constraints. don’t include customer identifiers in tags/labels. component_id + region + capability is safe.

Elliot Price: if you need “what do we say publicly” guidance, docs side has a short section under incident templates. but for tagging, stick to the schema + examples above.

Jae Kim: awesome. i’ll implement with `capability=streaming` on chat stream path + keep `route_name` internal. will ping if CI yells at me.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: if CI fails, it’ll usually point at invalid enum value. easiest fix is copy/paste from the registry.

Logan Wright: also worth checking the hotfix PR we landed last week (misclassified embeddings vs chat). the failure mode was exactly “wrong capability tag → wrong component update”. so thank you for being careful here 🙏

title: #eng-oncall: 1759902011
preview:
Christine Wong: dropping some quick on-call feedback on the status-page update runbook. Used it twice this week (Sev1 + a noisy Sev2) and it’s still a bit too manual during the first 10 mins.

Christine Wong: main pain: runbook says “verify impact on dashboards” but the links are scattered + we end up hunting for the right region/capability panels. during Sev1, we lost ~6-8 mins just clicking around.

Rafael Mendes: yep, want that kind of feedback. can you call out the specific steps that felt highest friction?

Christine Wong: sure.
1) step: identify component(s) impacted → we have to translate internal service names (gateway, scheduler) to public components (Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings) manually.
2) step: confirm region(s) impacted → runbook points to “component health dashboards” but doesn’t deep link per region.
3) step: post first update → template exists but still a bunch of copy/paste + adding components in statuspage UI.

Christine Wong: also the “double check approval” note is vague. in Sev1, i wasn’t sure if i needed Support lead before the first Investigating update or only once we mark Partial Outage.

Owen Phillips: +1 on approval ambiguity. in the moment i default to “ask in #support” and then we’re waiting on someone to respond.

Rafael Mendes: on approval: expectation is first Investigating update should not be blocked, esp for Sev1/Sev0. approvals apply when we’re changing states aggressively (e.g. resolved) or when the message includes customer-impact quantification that might be wrong. we should encode that more clearly.

Miles Thornton: for the “verify impact” step: could we have the runbook call out a single canonical dashboard landing page that has filters (component_id, region, capability) so you don’t chase links?

Aisha Bello: we do have a landing page but it’s not obvious. right now it’s under Observability → “Status Components”. i can add a short link + pre-set views for top combos (us-east streaming, eu-west api, etc.).

Christine Wong: that’d help. the most common path is “us-east + streaming” or “us-east + hosted api”. if the runbook had those links, would shave off time.

Allison Grant: also: we keep asking on-call to look at 3 different signals (error %, p95 latency, burn-rate). can we standardize the order + make it one panel that answers “should we mark degraded?”

Christine Wong: yes. right now the runbook says “check burn alerts” but then also “check p95” and “check support ticket volume” which is good but too much for TTFU.

Rafael Mendes: goal is: 5-min TTFU. we need “fast path” steps for Sev0/Sev1 and then “follow-up validation” steps after first update.

Devon Price: from incident-bot side, we can reduce manual steps:
- intake already has fields for component/region/capability (or will)
- we can auto-generate a draft Investigating update + push to statuspage with a confirmation
- we can auto-attach dashboard links based on those tags

Christine Wong: what i want in the heat of it: one command like `/incident status investigating` that:
1) pre-fills impacted components + region
2) includes 1-2 metrics placeholders (e.g. “elevated 5xx in us-east”) without forcing me to be precise
3) posts + logs in #incidents

Incident-bot: (bot) FYI: statuspage integration is in progress. See ENG-4822 / PR-19344.

Mina Haddad: on the mapping issue: it’s not just service→component, it’s capability split too. e.g. “chat completions OK but streaming disconnects spike” should hit Hosted API Streaming (us-east), not Hosted API (us-east). we need a quick reference in the runbook.

Christine Wong: exactly. we debated this mid-incident and it slowed us down. i’d prefer “default safe” guidance: if only streaming is broken, mark streaming component only; don’t mark the whole API unless non-stream requests impacted.

Allison Grant: we can also encode that in signals. streaming has specific disconnect/error counters now (or soon). when those fire, we can suggest the streaming component automatically.

Aisha Bello: i can update dashboards to include a “recommended component” callout (based on labels) but we should be careful about false positives.

Miles Thornton: runbook could have a Sev0/Sev1 checklist:
- (0) confirm severity + assign Comms Lead
- (1) pick component(s) + region(s) (use incident intake fields)
- (2) post Investigating within 5 min (no approval required)
- (3) validate signals + refine components within 15 min
- (4) add identified/mitigating updates as info becomes available

Owen Phillips: + add a “minimum viable investigating” template. on-call spends too long trying to write perfect copy.

Christine Wong: yes. template should explicitly say it’s OK to be vague early, e.g. “We’re investigating elevated errors impacting Streaming in us-east. Next update in 30 minutes or sooner.”

Rafael Mendes: agreed. we want consistent, not overly specific.

Christine Wong: another minor friction: statuspage UI component selection is a lot of clicking. if bot can post with components preselected, that’s huge.

Devon Price: that’s planned. PR-19405 adds a statuspage API client w/ idempotency + audit log. once we trust it, we can do “draft + publish” flow.

Allison Grant: can we add an “anti-flap” note to runbook fast path? we shouldn’t mark degraded if it’s a single 2-min blip, but during Sev1 it’s obvious. guidance right now is too generic.

Mina Haddad: maybe: if burn-rate alert fired OR sustained error% > threshold for 5 mins → ok to set degraded. otherwise use “investigating” without changing component state? (or keep component “operational” but add incident?)

Christine Wong: +1. i’m also confused by “incident without component impact” vs “component degraded.” we should just have one clear recommendation.

Rafael Mendes: action items i’m capturing:
- update runbook with Sev0/Sev1 fast path + clear approval rules (no approval for first Investigating)
- add canonical dashboard landing page + deep links for common region/capability
- add a mapping cheat sheet (service/capability → public component)
- incident-bot: command/flow to generate+publish investigating update w/ prefilled component+region

Aisha Bello: i’ll take dashboard landing + deep links. will post a shortlink in-thread once it’s ready.

Devon Price: i’ll sync w/ Rafael on bot flow. we can likely ship “draft text + components” first, then “one-click publish” after we’re confident in API retries/permissions.

Christine Wong: thanks. if we can shave even 5 mins off the first update, support tickets drop noticeably.

Owen Phillips: also add a tiny section: “if customers dispute status accuracy, do X” with a link to the escalation playbook. on-call ends up answering DMs otherwise.

Rafael Mendes: good call. will link escalation playbook + a one-liner “route to Support/CS; on-call provides dashboard evidence, not bespoke customer comms.”

Allison Grant: if you want, i can review the revised runbook text for clarity and consistency w/ signal standards.

Rafael Mendes: please do. i’ll post draft in Confluence and tag you + Christine for last-mile feedback.

- incident-bot: command/flow to generate+publish investigating update w/ prefilled component+region

Aisha Bello: i’ll take dashboard landing + deep links. will post a shortlink in-thread once it’s ready.

Devon Price: i’ll sync w/ Rafael on bot flow. we can likely ship “draft text + components” first, then “one-click publish” after we’re confident in API retries/permissions.

Christine Wong: thanks. if we can shave even 5 mins off the first update, support tickets drop noticeably.

Owen Phillips: also add a tiny section: “if customers dispute status accuracy, do X” with a link to the escalation playbook. on-call ends up answering DMs otherwise.

Rafael Mendes: good call. will link escalation playbook + a one-liner “route to Support/CS; on-call provides dashboard evidence, not bespoke customer comms.”

Allison Grant: if you want, i can review the revised runbook text for clarity and consistency w/ signal standards.

Rafael Mendes: please do. i’ll post draft in Confluence and tag you + Christine for last-mile feedback.

title: #eng-sre: 1759882210
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Kicking off a thread on status page component taxonomy. We need to stop calling everything “API” :grimacing:. Proposal: split Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings (and maybe Reranking) + split by region. Want feedback on shape + naming.

Christine Wong: +1. Current single “API” component is killing us in comms. Customers see “Operational” while embeddings is on fire.

Mina Haddad: For region split, do we want cloud region names (us-east-1/us-west-2/eu-west-1) or friendly (US East/US West/EU West)? Friendly is better for customers, but mapping has to be unambiguous.

Allison Grant: Observability side: we can tag metrics by `region` already; the gateway has `pop` and `cluster` labels. Friendly display name is fine as long as component_id uses canonical region key.

Aisha Bello: If we do product x region, number of components blows up quickly. What’s our target component count? Statuspage UI gets unwieldy >~25 components.

Rafael Mendes: Right now we’re thinking: only split regions for customer-facing request paths (Hosted API + Streaming + Embeddings + Reranking). Console stays global. Dedicated/Private handled separately/limited exposure.

Miles Thornton: +1 to keeping it mostly a 2D grid. Also recommend we treat Streaming as capability, not just “Chat”. It’s a distinct failure mode (disconnects, stalled tokens) even when non-streaming completions look fine.

Christine Wong: Draft list (WIP):
- Hosted API (US East)
- Hosted API (US West)
- Hosted API (EU West)
- Streaming (US East)
- Streaming (US West)
- Streaming (EU West)
- Embeddings (US East)
- Embeddings (US West)
- Embeddings (EU West)
- Reranking (US East)
- Reranking (US West)
- Reranking (EU West)
- Console (Global)

Mina Haddad: That’s 13 components. That feels manageable.

Aisha Bello: For Hosted API, are we mixing chat + text generation? If a model router issue breaks only chat routes, we’ll still over-report.

Rafael Mendes: Good q. My bias: keep Hosted API as “non-streaming text generation/chat request acceptance” and call out route-level details in incident updates. We can add a “Chat” component later if needed, but I’d rather not go to 20+ right away.

Allison Grant: We *can* separate chat vs completions based on route tags (`/v1/chat/completions` vs `/v1/completions`). But we’d need consistency across SDKs + gateway normalization.

Logan Wright: Gateway emits `route_name` today but it’s messy (some are templated, some are raw paths). If we make “Chat” a public component, we need clean cardinality + a mapping table.

Irene Choi: Infra perspective: splitting chat vs text gen might create more confusion unless we have super crisp definitions. Customers think “chat” = their product, not our endpoint.

Dev Patel: Support POV: the #1 complaint is “embeddings failing but status page green.” #2 is “streaming disconnects but you say API operational.” I haven’t seen many tickets asking for chat vs completions split.

Sean Gallagher: Agree w/ Dev. Let’s fix the high-signal pain first: embeddings + streaming as first-class components, split by region. We can iterate once we have accuracy audits.

Aisha Bello: For region grouping, can we align to the 3 regions customers commonly use: US East, US West, EU? What about APAC?

Mina Haddad: APAC usage exists but it’s mostly via partner/edge right now; traffic often routes to US West anyway. We can add APAC once we have a distinct region/control plane.

Allison Grant: From dashboards side, we currently have clean partitions for `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`. APAC would be “other” today. I’d rather not publish “Other”.

Christine Wong: Naming: “Hosted API” vs “API Requests” vs “Inference API”? Trying to avoid ambiguity w/ Dedicated.

Rafael Mendes: Proposal:
- “Hosted API” for standard request/response
- “Hosted Streaming” for SSE streaming
- “Hosted Embeddings”
- “Hosted Reranking”
Then in incident templates we’ll say “Hosted API (US East) experiencing elevated 5xx”.

Dev Patel: Please keep “Hosted” in the name. Otherwise enterprise customers think Dedicated is included in the same component.

Miles Thornton: +1, and component_ids should include deployment mode:
`hosted_api_us_east`, `hosted_streaming_us_east`, `hosted_embeddings_us_east`, etc.
Display names: “Hosted API (US East)”.

Logan Wright: Mapping rule suggestion (so we don’t fight this during incidents):
- If request has `capability=embeddings` => embeddings component
- Else if `streaming=true` => streaming component
- Else => hosted_api
Edge case: rerank is non-streaming but not “api” (customers care).

Allison Grant: Also need clear region selection rule:
- Use serving cluster region (where inference happened), not where customer originated.
Otherwise status can show US East impacted even if we failed over to US West.

Mina Haddad: Agree. Region should represent where we’re serving from / where capacity is impacted.

Aisha Bello: How do we handle partial failures (e.g., only some models)? We won’t have per-model components.

Sean Gallagher: That’s OK. Component degraded + incident text should specify “some models” / “subset of traffic” without listing customer names or capacity details.

Christine Wong: I’ll add definition blurbs:
- Hosted API: non-streaming generation + request acceptance
- Hosted Streaming: streaming responses (SSE) delivery and token streaming
- Hosted Embeddings: embedding generation
- Hosted Reranking: rerank endpoint

Dev Patel: Also add “what it does NOT include” for each (e.g., Console, Dedicated). Helps support macros.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
1) Christine to turn this into canonical taxonomy doc + component definitions.
2) Mina/Logan to propose region + mapping rules (cluster region).
3) Allison/Aisha to sanity check dashboards/alerts exist per component.
Goal: ready for PR on component registry this week.

Allison Grant: I’ll confirm the metric queries. We should include both error rate + latency p95 for “degraded” criteria, and hysteresis to avoid flapping.

Miles Thornton: Also think about grouping on Statuspage UI: group by product (Hosted API/Streaming/Embeddings/Reranking) then by region underneath.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Let’s proceed with the 13-component list. If someone feels strongly about adding “Chat” as a separate component, propose it with ticket + evidence.

Christine Wong: Cool, I’ll draft the doc and post here for review EOD. (Will include the component_id naming + ownership placeholders.)

title: #architecture: 1749014421
preview:
Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gateway endpoint attached to private route tables
- ECR API + ECR DKR: Interface endpoints
- STS: Interface endpoint
- CloudWatch Logs: Interface endpoint
- KMS: Interface endpoint (optional?)
- EC2 + Autoscaling + ELB: optional / depends on if they run EKS private endpoint + node lifecycle needs
- SSM: optional (nice-to-have for ops)

Enable `private_dns_enabled=true` on interface endpoints by default.

Hanae Suzuki: DNS gotchas: enabling private DNS for ECR endpoints can conflict if customer has a private hosted zone for `ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com` (seen in some locked-down shops). But I think default ON is still correct, just document override.

Simran Kaur: In reference architecture doc we say “no public egress required for image pulls” if endpoints exist. That implicitly assumes private DNS is enabled, otherwise resolving `*.dkr.ecr...` still goes public, right?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. Without private DNS, clients need to use the endpoint-specific DNS names (the long `vpce-xxxx` names). Nobody does that for ECR.

Sean Gallagher: Operationally: installer should fail fast if it can’t pull images / reach control plane deps. Don’t wait until half the cluster is up.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should bake preflight checks into installer (or separate “network verify” command). Like: “from inside subnet, can you resolve and connect to ECR, STS, S3?”.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?
1) AWS API validation (DescribeVpcEndpoints + attributes)
2) If installer is running on an instance in the VPC (common in enterprise), do runtime DNS/connectivity checks.

Victor Alvarez: That’s what I was thinking.

Marcus Lin: On “private DNS enabled by default”: +1, but we should also validate VPC flags: `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames`. If those are off, private DNS won’t work even if endpoints exist.

Victor Alvarez: Good call. Terraform module can set those on the VPC resource, but if customer is using an existing VPC we need to either enforce or document.

Kira Thompson: Docs request: spell out “if you bring-your-own VPC, you must have enableDnsSupport/Hostnames = true” and “if you have custom DHCP options / on-prem DNS, check resolver rules.” This comes up every discovery.

Simran Kaur: Also EKS private endpoint mode: for private cluster, you still need certain AWS APIs reachable from nodes (ECR/STS/EC2). If we don’t include EC2 endpoint, node bootstrap can break depending on how hard they lock egress.

Nina Petrova: For Redwood Private, we assume EKS (at least in alpha reference arch). Nodes definitely call EC2 APIs during bootstrapping + CNI. If no NAT, you need more endpoints than just ECR.

Victor Alvarez: So maybe the “required” endpoint set for alpha should be bigger:
- ecr.api
- ecr.dkr
- s3 (gateway)
- sts
- logs
- ec2
And “recommended”:
- kms (if KMS-encrypted stuff)
- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

Marcus Lin: Careful with “required”: if they allow NAT egress, they can skip endpoints. But for “no public egress” posture, yes, that’s the required set.

Sean Gallagher: Can we encode that as installer output?
- Detect NAT route to IGW? (well, NAT implies route to NAT in private RT)
- If no NAT present, then enforce endpoints set
- If NAT present, warn if endpoints missing but don’t fail

Bruno Silva: NAT detection via AWS APIs is possible (describe route tables for `0.0.0.0/0 -> nat-...`). Not perfect but good.

Rafael Mendes: Another check: verify endpoints are in the same subnets as the cluster nodes (or at least reachable). We’ve seen endpoints created only in one AZ/subnet and then cross-AZ traffic gets weird / costs.

Victor Alvarez: Module default will create interface endpoints in all private subnets (one per AZ). But yes, installer can verify `SubnetIds` covers expected.

Hanae Suzuki: Endpoint SG rules: we should keep it simple. The endpoint ENIs should allow 443 inbound from node SG (or from private subnet CIDRs). Least privilege is nice, but if we get it wrong we strand people.

Marcus Lin: Least privilege suggestion: allow 443 from “cluster nodes SG” + “installer/bastion SG” if provided. Avoid CIDR-wide if we can. But we need an escape hatch.

Irene Choi: For alpha, I’d rather have a working default than ultra-tight SG logic that’s fragile. But we can still do SG-to-SG.

Victor Alvarez: Proposed endpoint SG default:
- Ingress: tcp/443 from `var.allowed_client_security_group_ids` (default: node SG, and optionally a shared “private clients” SG)
- Egress: allow all (AWS endpoints are managed anyway)

Kira Thompson: How do we explain private DNS risk to customers? We should include a doc note:
- If you already manage private hosted zones for `amazonaws.com` names, set `private_dns_enabled=false` and configure Route53 resolver rules accordingly.

Simran Kaur: + link to AWS docs on endpoint private DNS + split-horizon. (People mix it up with “private hosted zone”.)

Nina Petrova: Installer-side: please add explicit error strings. Something like:
"Missing VPC endpoint for ECR (com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr). If your private subnets have no NAT egress, image pulls will fail. Fix: enable VPC endpoint + private DNS or add NAT/proxy."

Sean Gallagher: Can we include a one-liner command in the error that works from inside cluster?
`nslookup <acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.

and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.

Aisha Rahman: Make it explicit: no break-glass for contractor. Also confirm audit export is available for exception window.
Hiro Tanaka: From SE side, what does the contractor need? Usually just health, deploy status, and admin audit log view.

title: #eng-infra: 1748572103
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: kicking off a quick thread to de-risk bounds implementation before we merge the autoscaler changes. Need alignment on (1) edge cases + semantics (2) defaulting strategy for existing Dedicated deployments (3) who owns migration/backfill. I’m looking at ENG-22140 + ENG-22153.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. I’m in the autoscaler loop code now. Biggest question: do we expect control plane to always send explicit `min_replicas` / `max_replicas`, or can they be null? i can clamp either way but need deterministic behavior.

Benji Okafor: schema PR (ENG-22141) is leaning nullable fields: `bounds_enabled` + `min_replicas?` + `max_replicas?` + cooldowns. Validation: if bounds_enabled=true then require both min/max and enforce min<=max. If bounds_enabled=false, we treat min/max as ignored even if present.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine POV, we can compute an “effective policy” snapshot that the autoscaler reads. Proposal:
- if feature flag off OR bounds_enabled=false => `effective_min`/`effective_max` = empty, autoscaler uses legacy behavior
- if bounds_enabled=true => clamp desired replicas into [min,max]
We should also emit a reason string for audit/logging: `clamp:min` / `clamp:max` / `no-bounds`.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok but we still need to decide what happens to *existing* deployments when we first roll out schema. we can’t suddenly require min/max everywhere.

Michael Nguyen: on migration/backfill: I can add columns/fields with null defaults and backfill policy rows in a “legacy mode” so reads don’t break. But we need to decide what value to set for existing deployments in DB.

Ethan Park: strong preference: do NOT backfill min/max for existing customers. Too risky (we’ll guess wrong). Instead:
- add new policy object w/ bounds_enabled=false by default
- only set bounds_enabled=true when explicitly enabled via console/api + feature flag
That preserves behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: works for me as long as autoscaler treats “no policy / legacy policy” identically. I’ll code defensively: if policy missing or bounds_enabled=false => skip clamp.

Rafael Mendes: SRE lens: please avoid any migration that changes fleet behavior without explicit enablement. If we do backfill, it should be a no-op. Also we need a clear story on “max below current replicas” if a customer tightens bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, edge cases list i want to cover:
1) min > max (should be rejected)
2) max < current_replicas on update
3) min > current_replicas on update
4) min=0 (do we allow scale-to-zero?)
5) predictor missing/stale; predictive wants 0 or null
6) region shortage: can’t meet min

Benji Okafor: (1) will be API validation error 400 w/ field-level message.
Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.
