title: #eng-oncall: 1747684422
preview:
Tomáš Novák: doing a quick dry-run of the KMS decrypt failures runbook in staging before we publish. goal: follow it literally + note anything confusing/missing. staging target: `stg-usw2` control-plane + one data-plane worker pool.

Tomáš Novák: scenario A we’re simulating: intermittent KMS timeouts + some throttles. I’m going to inject failures via the test harness. please drop notes as you go.

PagerDuty Bot: :rotating_light: TEST EVENT: "KMS dependency degraded" triggered in STAGING (service=control-plane, env=stg-usw2). Routed to #eng-oncall.

Sean Gallagher: ok following runbook as-written. first issue: it says “check KMS dashboard” but doesn’t link it. I found it via Observability Pack -> Dependencies -> KMS, but we should paste the actual URL.

Allison Grant: +1. also the dashboard name changed last week (PR-844). it’s now “KMS / Envelope Encryption (platform)” not “KMS health”.

Kira Thompson: noted. I’ll add exact link + update screenshot refs. can someone paste the canonical grafana path?

Allison Grant: try: `https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/deps-kms/kms-envelope-encryption` (staging uses same UID). If not accessible, link to “search: deps-kms”.

Tomáš Novák: injecting now. expect p95 kms latency spikes + some `DeadlineExceeded` in logs.

Caleb Johnson: runbook step 1 says “look for `kms.error_code` in traces”. but in Honeycomb the field is `kms.error.kind` + `kms.provider_code` (per PR-18515). we should align naming.

Kevin Osei: yeah error taxonomy landed as `kms.error.kind` values: `Timeout`, `Throttling`, `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `Unknown`. `kms.provider_code` carries AWS/GCP/Azure code.

Kira Thompson: can you both paste the exact field names + example query so I can drop it into the runbook?

Caleb Johnson: traces query example:
`service.name = "control-plane" AND span.name = "kms.decrypt" AND kms.error.kind != ""`
then break down by `kms.error.kind` + `deployment.region`.

Sean Gallagher: runbook step 2 (logs) is a bit vague. it says “check structured logs for decrypt failures” but not the grep. I’m using:
`kubectl -n control-plane logs deploy/api --since=15m | rg 'kms' | rg 'decrypt|envelope'`
But better is to use log explorer with fields.

Allison Grant: agree. preferred: log explorer query by `event="kms_call"` OR `component="kms"` and filter `kms.error.kind`. also we should include the request-id field name (`req_id`) to correlate with 5xx.

Tomáš Novák: here’s an example log line from staging while injection is on:
```
{"level":"warn","msg":"kms decrypt failed","component":"kms","kms.error.kind":"Timeout","kms.provider_code":"DeadlineExceeded","kms.key_arn":"arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:***:key/9c1f...","retry.attempt":2,"retry.remaining_budget_ms":120,"latency_ms":980,"req_id":"01JVE7B7V5G7...","tenant":"internal-staging"}
```

Sean Gallagher: perfect. we should paste that into runbook (redacted) + explain what’s actionable.

Rafael Mendes: following mitigation section: it says “enable degrade mode” but doesn’t say where/what. is that the circuit breaker flag in control-plane config?

Tomáš Novák: yep. it’s the `kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true` (should already be default) + the “fail-fast after open” behavior is controlled by `kms.circuit_breaker.open_fail_fast=true`. runbook should say how to confirm it’s actually open.

Allison Grant: we have metrics for that: `redwood_kms_circuit_breaker_state{state="open"}` (0/1 gauge). add to runbook.

Kevin Osei: also add the retry budget metric. in the near-miss we saw retry amplification. now we have `redwood_kms_retry_budget_exhausted_total` and `redwood_kms_retry_attempts_total`.

Sean Gallagher: another confusion: runbook says “if timeout spike, reduce retries” but doesn’t give the safe knob + bounds. do we want to reference the guardrails from PR-18502?

Connor O'Brien: +1. for oncall we should provide the exact config path + a warning about overrides. otherwise people will go hunting.

Tomáš Novák: suggestion for runbook text:
- first try: lower `kms.retry.max_attempts` to 1 (staging/prod via config service)
- keep `kms.timeout_ms` at 1000 (don’t raise)
- if we must override guardrails, requires #eng-releases + SRE approval
We should confirm the current prod defaults though.

Allison Grant: defaults are: `timeout_ms=1000`, `max_attempts=2`, exponential backoff w/ jitter, overall cap via `retry_budget_ms=1500`. (from PR-18502 + hotfix PR-18563).

Kira Thompson: ok adding a “Safe default knobs” box + callout for the approval requirement.

Hanae Suzuki: security note: runbook should differentiate “KMS outage/degraded” vs “InvalidCiphertext / key material mismatch”. mitigation is different. right now it lumps them under “decrypt failures”.

Hanae Suzuki: for `InvalidCiphertext`: do NOT suggest “just retry”. it’s usually wrong key ARN, wrong key version, or cutover state mismatch. guidance should include checking cutover flags and dual-read window.

Sean Gallagher: +1. I hit that in the doc: “if invalid ciphertext, rotate keys” is scary. better: “pause cutover, enable dual-read, validate key IDs, escalate to Security + Platform”.

Tomáš Novák: agreed. in this dry run we’re only on timeouts, but we should edit that section now.

Kevin Osei: also for `AccessDenied`: runbook should say to check IAM role ARN and recent terraform changes. most common is missing `kms:Decrypt` on the correct key resource, or using the rotation principal for runtime decrypt (role separation).

Caleb Johnson: traces side: add a note that `AccessDenied` errors are usually fast (low latency) vs timeouts, so on dashboards you’ll see error rate up w/o latency spike. could help differentiate.

Rafael Mendes: I’m at step “verify scope / blast radius”. runbook says “check if impacts hosted vs private” but doesn’t say how. maybe add: look at `deployment.mode` tag in metrics? do we have it?

Allison Grant: we tag metrics with `env` + `region` + `service`. not `deployment.mode` everywhere yet. best short-term: use “tenant” or “cluster” breakdown (private clusters have `cluster_name=priv-*`). add that as heuristic.

Kira Thompson: will add a table:
- Hosted/Dedicated: clusters `core-*`
- Private: clusters `priv-*` (or customer-named)
And point to the “KMS health dashboard -> by cluster” panel.

Tomáš Novák: continuing: I see elevated 5xx in staging API. runbook says “confirm this is KMS-derived 5xx” — we should include the metric query for that.

Sean Gallagher: I used:
`sum(rate(http_server_requests_total{status=~"5..",service="api"}[5m])) by (route)`
then correlate with `sum(rate(redwood_kms_errors_total[5m])) by (kms_error_kind)`.

Allison Grant: metric names in our stack are slightly different (prom):
- `redwood_http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}`
- `redwood_kms_errors_total{error_kind="Timeout"}`
Let’s include both prom + grafana panel pointer.

Tomáš Novák: ok, injection off. system recovered in ~3m once throttle stopped. circuit breaker opened briefly but we didn’t page again.

PagerDuty Bot: RESOLVED: TEST EVENT "KMS dependency degraded" in STAGING. Duration: 7m 12s.

Connor O'Brien: runbook “Escalation” section: needs concrete who/where. i.e. tag #eng-security for `InvalidCiphertext`/key mismatch, #eng-infra for IAM/policy, #eng-sre for dependency health. also include “when to open incident” thresholds.

Sean Gallagher: yes. add paging guidance:
- page SRE if `redwood_kms_errors_total` > X for Y mins OR `p95 latency` > threshold
- open incident if customer impact confirmed (prod) or if decrypt failures exceed 0.5% for 10m
(we can tune numbers but need *something*).

Allison Grant: I’ll propose thresholds consistent w/ PR-844 tuning notes: warn at 0.1% decrypt failures, page at 0.5% for 10m (prod only). staging should be non-paging.

Hanae Suzuki: also add explicit “Do not disable envelope encryption” statement. we had someone suggest it during a past incident. the runbook should say it’s not an approved mitigation.

Kira Thompson: adding “Not approved mitigations” section:
- disabling encryption
- widening IAM to `kms:*`
- extending retry budgets without approval

Kevin Osei: can we add an “evidence” note? even for incidents, capture req_ids + sample logs + config changes + who approved. helps later audits.

Tomáš Novák: good call. maybe link the rotation/evidence template but at least say “paste links in incident doc”.

Caleb Johnson: small doc nit: the runbook references `/healthz` but new endpoint is `/_internal/kms/health` (PR-18527). add curl example:
`curl -sS http://localhost:8080/_internal/kms/health | jq`

Sean Gallagher: another nit: needs a pre-req at top: “you need access to config service + grafana + log explorer”. I wasted 2 mins figuring out which kube context.

Kira Thompson: got it. I’ll add a “Prereqs / access” block + kube context examples:
- `kubectx stg-usw2-core`
- `kubens control-plane`

Tomáš Novák: summary of edits from dry-run:
1) Add direct links to KMS dashboard + panels (Allison)
2) Align field names (`kms.error.kind`, `kms.provider_code`, `req_id`) + example queries (Caleb/Kevin)
3) Clarify degrade mode/circuit breaker confirmation + metric (Allison)
4) Add safe knob guidance for retries/timeouts + guardrail note (Sean/Connor)
5) Split guidance by error kind: Timeout/Throttling vs AccessDenied vs InvalidCiphertext (Hanae/Kevin)
6) Add cluster heuristics for hosted vs private impact (Kira)
7) Update healthcheck endpoint path + curl example (Caleb)
8) Add escalation + paging thresholds + “not approved mitigations” + evidence capture note (everyone)

Tomáš Novák: Kira, want me to open a doc PR / confluence edit request or are you making the edits directly?

Kira Thompson: I’ll take first pass edits in Confluence today, then ping back here for final review. If you can paste any missing links (grafana + log explorer) that’d help.

Allison Grant: I’ll DM you the grafana links + the exact metric names from the dashboard JSON.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a short section to the runbook about “how to tell if this is a platform-wide KMS issue vs a single tenant/cluster” and send text.

Hanae Suzuki: I’ll draft the `InvalidCiphertext` / cutover / dual-read guidance (incl “pause cutover” steps) and send to Kira.

Tomáš Novák: cool. once updated, we’ll do a second dry run next week (include `AccessDenied` simulation maybe) before publishing widely.
text:
Tomáš Novák: doing a quick dry-run of the KMS decrypt failures runbook in staging before we publish. goal: follow it literally + note anything confusing/missing. staging target: `stg-usw2` control-plane + one data-plane worker pool.

Tomáš Novák: scenario A we’re simulating: intermittent KMS timeouts + some throttles. I’m going to inject failures via the test harness. please drop notes as you go.

PagerDuty Bot: :rotating_light: TEST EVENT: "KMS dependency degraded" triggered in STAGING (service=control-plane, env=stg-usw2). Routed to #eng-oncall.

Sean Gallagher: ok following runbook as-written. first issue: it says “check KMS dashboard” but doesn’t link it. I found it via Observability Pack -> Dependencies -> KMS, but we should paste the actual URL.

Allison Grant: +1. also the dashboard name changed last week (PR-844). it’s now “KMS / Envelope Encryption (platform)” not “KMS health”.

Kira Thompson: noted. I’ll add exact link + update screenshot refs. can someone paste the canonical grafana path?

Allison Grant: try: `https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/deps-kms/kms-envelope-encryption` (staging uses same UID). If not accessible, link to “search: deps-kms”.

Tomáš Novák: injecting now. expect p95 kms latency spikes + some `DeadlineExceeded` in logs.

Caleb Johnson: runbook step 1 says “look for `kms.error_code` in traces”. but in Honeycomb the field is `kms.error.kind` + `kms.provider_code` (per PR-18515). we should align naming.

Kevin Osei: yeah error taxonomy landed as `kms.error.kind` values: `Timeout`, `Throttling`, `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `Unknown`. `kms.provider_code` carries AWS/GCP/Azure code.

Kira Thompson: can you both paste the exact field names + example query so I can drop it into the runbook?

Caleb Johnson: traces query example:
`service.name = "control-plane" AND span.name = "kms.decrypt" AND kms.error.kind != ""`
then break down by `kms.error.kind` + `deployment.region`.

Sean Gallagher: runbook step 2 (logs) is a bit vague. it says “check structured logs for decrypt failures” but not the grep. I’m using:
`kubectl -n control-plane logs deploy/api --since=15m | rg 'kms' | rg 'decrypt|envelope'`
But better is to use log explorer with fields.

Allison Grant: agree. preferred: log explorer query by `event="kms_call"` OR `component="kms"` and filter `kms.error.kind`. also we should include the request-id field name (`req_id`) to correlate with 5xx.

Tomáš Novák: here’s an example log line from staging while injection is on:
```
{"level":"warn","msg":"kms decrypt failed","component":"kms","kms.error.kind":"Timeout","kms.provider_code":"DeadlineExceeded","kms.key_arn":"arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:***:key/9c1f...","retry.attempt":2,"retry.remaining_budget_ms":120,"latency_ms":980,"req_id":"01JVE7B7V5G7...","tenant":"internal-staging"}
```

Sean Gallagher: perfect. we should paste that into runbook (redacted) + explain what’s actionable.

Rafael Mendes: following mitigation section: it says “enable degrade mode” but doesn’t say where/what. is that the circuit breaker flag in control-plane config?

Tomáš Novák: yep. it’s the `kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true` (should already be default) + the “fail-fast after open” behavior is controlled by `kms.circuit_breaker.open_fail_fast=true`. runbook should say how to confirm it’s actually open.

Allison Grant: we have metrics for that: `redwood_kms_circuit_breaker_state{state="open"}` (0/1 gauge). add to runbook.

Kevin Osei: also add the retry budget metric. in the near-miss we saw retry amplification. now we have `redwood_kms_retry_budget_exhausted_total` and `redwood_kms_retry_attempts_total`.

Sean Gallagher: another confusion: runbook says “if timeout spike, reduce retries” but doesn’t give the safe knob + bounds. do we want to reference the guardrails from PR-18502?

Connor O'Brien: +1. for oncall we should provide the exact config path + a warning about overrides. otherwise people will go hunting.

Tomáš Novák: suggestion for runbook text:
- first try: lower `kms.retry.max_attempts` to 1 (staging/prod via config service)
- keep `kms.timeout_ms` at 1000 (don’t raise)
- if we must override guardrails, requires #eng-releases + SRE approval
We should confirm the current prod defaults though.

Allison Grant: defaults are: `timeout_ms=1000`, `max_attempts=2`, exponential backoff w/ jitter, overall cap via `retry_budget_ms=1500`. (from PR-18502 + hotfix PR-18563).

Kira Thompson: ok adding a “Safe default knobs” box + callout for the approval requirement.

Hanae Suzuki: security note: runbook should differentiate “KMS outage/degraded” vs “InvalidCiphertext / key material mismatch”. mitigation is different. right now it lumps them under “decrypt failures”.

Hanae Suzuki: for `InvalidCiphertext`: do NOT suggest “just retry”. it’s usually wrong key ARN, wrong key version, or cutover state mismatch. guidance should include checking cutover flags and dual-read window.

Sean Gallagher: +1. I hit that in the doc: “if invalid ciphertext, rotate keys” is scary. better: “pause cutover, enable dual-read, validate key IDs, escalate to Security + Platform”.

Tomáš Novák: agreed. in this dry run we’re only on timeouts, but we should edit that section now.

Kevin Osei: also for `AccessDenied`: runbook should say to check IAM role ARN and recent terraform changes. most common is missing `kms:Decrypt` on the correct key resource, or using the rotation principal for runtime decrypt (role separation).

Caleb Johnson: traces side: add a note that `AccessDenied` errors are usually fast (low latency) vs timeouts, so on dashboards you’ll see error rate up w/o latency spike. could help differentiate.

Rafael Mendes: I’m at step “verify scope / blast radius”. runbook says “check if impacts hosted vs private” but doesn’t say how. maybe add: look at `deployment.mode` tag in metrics? do we have it?

Allison Grant: we tag metrics with `env` + `region` + `service`. not `deployment.mode` everywhere yet. best short-term: use “tenant” or “cluster” breakdown (private clusters have `cluster_name=priv-*`). add that as heuristic.

Kira Thompson: will add a table:
- Hosted/Dedicated: clusters `core-*`
- Private: clusters `priv-*` (or customer-named)
And point to the “KMS health dashboard -> by cluster” panel.

Tomáš Novák: continuing: I see elevated 5xx in staging API. runbook says “confirm this is KMS-derived 5xx” — we should include the metric query for that.

Sean Gallagher: I used:
`sum(rate(http_server_requests_total{status=~"5..",service="api"}[5m])) by (route)`
then correlate with `sum(rate(redwood_kms_errors_total[5m])) by (kms_error_kind)`.

Allison Grant: metric names in our stack are slightly different (prom):
- `redwood_http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}`
- `redwood_kms_errors_total{error_kind="Timeout"}`
Let’s include both prom + grafana panel pointer.

Tomáš Novák: ok, injection off. system recovered in ~3m once throttle stopped. circuit breaker opened briefly but we didn’t page again.

PagerDuty Bot: RESOLVED: TEST EVENT "KMS dependency degraded" in STAGING. Duration: 7m 12s.

Connor O'Brien: runbook “Escalation” section: needs concrete who/where. i.e. tag #eng-security for `InvalidCiphertext`/key mismatch, #eng-infra for IAM/policy, #eng-sre for dependency health. also include “when to open incident” thresholds.

Sean Gallagher: yes. add paging guidance:
- page SRE if `redwood_kms_errors_total` > X for Y mins OR `p95 latency` > threshold
- open incident if customer impact confirmed (prod) or if decrypt failures exceed 0.5% for 10m
(we can tune numbers but need *something*).

Allison Grant: I’ll propose thresholds consistent w/ PR-844 tuning notes: warn at 0.1% decrypt failures, page at 0.5% for 10m (prod only). staging should be non-paging.

Hanae Suzuki: also add explicit “Do not disable envelope encryption” statement. we had someone suggest it during a past incident. the runbook should say it’s not an approved mitigation.

Kira Thompson: adding “Not approved mitigations” section:
- disabling encryption
- widening IAM to `kms:*`
- extending retry budgets without approval

Kevin Osei: can we add an “evidence” note? even for incidents, capture req_ids + sample logs + config changes + who approved. helps later audits.

Tomáš Novák: good call. maybe link the rotation/evidence template but at least say “paste links in incident doc”.

Caleb Johnson: small doc nit: the runbook references `/healthz` but new endpoint is `/_internal/kms/health` (PR-18527). add curl example:
`curl -sS http://localhost:8080/_internal/kms/health | jq`

Sean Gallagher: another nit: needs a pre-req at top: “you need access to config service + grafana + log explorer”. I wasted 2 mins figuring out which kube context.

Kira Thompson: got it. I’ll add a “Prereqs / access” block + kube context examples:
- `kubectx stg-usw2-core`
- `kubens control-plane`

Tomáš Novák: summary of edits from dry-run:
1) Add direct links to KMS dashboard + panels (Allison)
2) Align field names (`kms.error.kind`, `kms.provider_code`, `req_id`) + example queries (Caleb/Kevin)
3) Clarify degrade mode/circuit breaker confirmation + metric (Allison)
4) Add safe knob guidance for retries/timeouts + guardrail note (Sean/Connor)
5) Split guidance by error kind: Timeout/Throttling vs AccessDenied vs InvalidCiphertext (Hanae/Kevin)
6) Add cluster heuristics for hosted vs private impact (Kira)
7) Update healthcheck endpoint path + curl example (Caleb)
8) Add escalation + paging thresholds + “not approved mitigations” + evidence capture note (everyone)

Tomáš Novák: Kira, want me to open a doc PR / confluence edit request or are you making the edits directly?

Kira Thompson: I’ll take first pass edits in Confluence today, then ping back here for final review. If you can paste any missing links (grafana + log explorer) that’d help.

Allison Grant: I’ll DM you the grafana links + the exact metric names from the dashboard JSON.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll add a short section to the runbook about “how to tell if this is a platform-wide KMS issue vs a single tenant/cluster” and send text.

Hanae Suzuki: I’ll draft the `InvalidCiphertext` / cutover / dual-read guidance (incl “pause cutover” steps) and send to Kira.

Tomáš Novák: cool. once updated, we’ll do a second dry run next week (include `AccessDenied` simulation maybe) before publishing widely.

title: #eng-oncall: 1747334700
preview:
Tomáš Novák: quick oncall feedback from last night: we *need* runbook URLs + “what to do first” steps embedded in the alert annotations. right now half the private min-pack alerts are basically “X is bad” and we’re hunting confluence + grafana manually.

Tomáš Novák: concrete ask for all page-worthy alerts (sev1/sev2):
1) `runbook_url` in annotations
2) `what_to_do_first` (2-4 bullets, copy/pasteable)
3) ideally `grafana_dashboard` link (even if it’s a generic landing dashboard)

PagerDuty: Incident note added by Tomáš Novák (eng-oncall): “Alert annotations missing runbook links / first steps. Slows triage.”

Rafael Mendes: +1. when the page fires at 3am, we don’t have time to reverse engineer what the author *meant*. also: include “is this customer action or redwood action” in the first steps if possible.

Aisha Bello: agree. also helps with paging hygiene: if the annotation tells you “check recording rule X is stale” vs “this is customer cluster resource pressure”, it reduces false escalations.

Sergio Costa: makes sense. we can add annotations in the prometheus rules bundle. question: do we want a standard template? (so we don’t have 10 different styles)

Allison Grant: yes please. we already have a standards blurb but not enforced. propose we standardize on:
- `summary`
- `description`
- `runbook_url`
- `grafana_url`
- `triage`
where `triage` is a short multi-line block.

Dylan Brooks: for grafana links: in private installs the base URL varies. we can’t hardcode `grafana.redwood.ai/...` like hosted. but we *can* link to dashboard UID and let them sub in base, or use the grafana variable we set in helm values (`global.grafana.externalUrl`), then use it in annotations via templating (if we have that in the rules deploy pipeline).

Tomáš Novák: even a UID + “open dashboard: Redwood Runtime Overview” is better than nothing. but if we can do `{{ $externalURL }}`-style in alertmanager, let’s do that.

Rafael Mendes: fwiw alertmanager supports `external_url`, but that’s alertmanager’s own URL. doesn’t help for grafana.

Sergio Costa: ok, proposal: annotate with a relative path like `/d/redwood-runtime-overview` + a note: “Grafana base URL is customer-specific; see install values `observability.grafana.externalUrl`”.

Kira Thompson: I can update the runbooks so the URL is stable + includes anchors for “What to do first” sections. if we’re embedding runbook links, we should avoid linking to headings that might change every edit.

Allison Grant: +1, stable anchors are a pain. better: link to the runbook page root and keep the first steps in the alert annotation itself.

Tomáš Novák: exactly. keep it to first actions + quick checks. runbook is for deeper.

Aisha Bello: also include a hint for SLO burn alerts: “check if this is global or single route/model” and “verify this isn’t a scrape gap”. we’ve paged on missing data before.

Sergio Costa: can you all sanity check this annotation format?

Sergio Costa:
```
annotations:
  summary: "High error rate for {{ $labels.service }} (Private)"
  description: "5xx errors are elevated for the Redwood Private {{ $labels.service }} service."
  runbook_url: "https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIVATE/Private+Observability+Day-2+Runbook"
  triage: |
    What to do first:
    1) Check if Prometheus is scraping the target: `up{job=\"{{ $labels.job }}\"}`
    2) Check recent deploys/restarts: `kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace=\"redwood\"}`
    3) Open dashboard: Redwood Control Plane Health (UID: rw-private-control-plane)
    4) If customer-managed ingress/auth, verify upstream 401/403 aren’t miscounted as 5xx
```

Rafael Mendes: this is good. add one more line for “if data is missing” -> “check `prometheus_tsdb_head_series` / scrape errors” (we see networkpolicy issues a lot).

Dylan Brooks: and for the dashboard line, use consistent naming: “Dashboard: Private / Control Plane / Health (uid=...)”. we’ll match the catalog.

Kira Thompson: runbook link is fine. we should pick a canonical page per alert family though (latency/availability vs gpu saturation vs log pipeline). otherwise everything links to day-2 runbook and it becomes a junk drawer.

Tomáš Novák: +1. categories:
- latency + availability -> SLO guidance page
- gpu saturation/oom -> runtime troubleshooting runbook
- scrape/tsdb -> infra prometheus/grafana runbook
- log forwarder -> log pipeline runbook

Allison Grant: ok, let’s tie to existing pages:
- SLO: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGSRE/Private+Install+SLO+Guidance
- Infra: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGINFRA/Prometheus+Grafana+for+Private+Installations
- Day2 ops: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIVATE/Private+Observability+Day-2+Runbook
- Audit/logging: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SEC/Private+Observability+and+Audit+Log+Interactions

Sergio Costa: got it. I’ll patch the rules in the observability-pack bundle to include:
- `runbook_url`
- `triage`
- dashboard UID reference

Sergio Costa: question: do we want `triage` for ticket-only alerts too? or only paging?

Aisha Bello: only paging required, but low effort to include for the high-frequency ticket ones too. just keep it short so it doesn’t blow up slack/pager payload.

Rafael Mendes: +1. also ensure we don’t include anything that could leak request payloads etc. keep it purely operational.

Tomáš Novák: perfect. can we also add a line in annotations like “Escalation: if persists 15m after rollout rollback -> page SRE”?

Allison Grant: careful. escalation policy varies by customer/support tier. but we can say “If unresolved after basic checks, follow escalation section in runbook.”

Dylan Brooks: implementation detail: prometheus rules are shipped as configmaps. long annotations increase object size but we’re fine. just don’t paste huge log snippets.

Sergio Costa: ack. I’ll keep `triage` <= ~10 lines.

Kira Thompson: if you share the list of alert names you’re touching, I’ll make sure the runbook pages have a matching “Alert reference” section so support can search by alertname.

Sergio Costa: will do. starting with:
- `PrivateAPIDown`
- `PrivateLatencySLOBurn`
- `PrivateHigh5xxRate`
- `PrivateGPUSaturation`
- `PrivateOOMKills`
- `PrivatePrometheusScrapeFailing`
- `PrivateLogForwarderDropping`

Tomáš Novák: awesome. thanks. once merged, can we backport to the release branch? oncall would love it before GA.

Rafael Mendes: +1 please. this is the kind of thing that makes “supported minimum” actually supportable.

Sergio Costa: yep. I’ll open PR today and tag @Rafael Mendes @Allison Grant for review.

Tomáš Novák: :thumbsup: (also: if we can, add a tiny “silence guidance” note for noisy alerts? even “don’t silence unless X”)

Aisha Bello: maybe later. for now: runbook link + first steps will already reduce panic silencing.

Tomáš Novák: fair. closing the loop once PR is up.

title: #eng: 1746816155
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Heads up: airgap bundle from the latest private installer build is missing the Grafana dashboards for the Private observability min pack. On-prem install comes up, Prometheus scrapes OK, but Grafana has *zero* Redwood dashboards.

Fatima Noor: Confirmed on my side. Using the onprem offline flow (no egress). Grafana is reachable, datasource is configured, but dashboard folder is empty. I only see default Grafana stuff.

Diego Santos: Ugh. Is it just dashboards or also alert rules?

Fatima Noor: Alerts *seem* present (I see the PrometheusRule objects). The missing bit looks like the dashboard JSONs aren’t in the airgap tarball at all.

Dylan Brooks: That matches what I saw in staging earlier this week. The Helm chart references dashboards via ConfigMaps, but the installer’s airgap bundle needs to include the `observability-pack` dashboards directory (or the generated ConfigMap manifests). Might be a manifest omission in the bundler.

Chloe Martin: Quick check: are we expecting the installer to ship raw dashboard JSONs and render ConfigMaps at install-time, or ship pre-rendered ConfigMaps? I thought we went with “ship raw dashboards + grafana sidecar loads them”.

Diego Santos: For Private installer we’re packaging the raw JSON artifacts + the helm bits. If those JSONs aren’t in the bundle, nothing to mount, so sidecar has nothing to load.

Connor O'Brien: I unpacked the artifact and searched:
`tar -tf redwood-private-airgap-bundle.tgz | grep -i grafana`
Nothing except the Grafana image tar.

Rafael Mendes: That explains the Support thread we saw last week (“dashboards blank after upgrade”). If this is the same root cause, we need a hotfix fast.

Sahana Iyer: CI didn’t catch this because smoke tests run in connected env and pull dashboards from the repo path. Airgap path uses the bundle manifest only. We should add an assertion that the bundle contains at least N dashboard JSON files.

Logan Wright: +1. Also can we verify the bundle includes the alert rules + recording rules too? Would rather not fix dashboards and then find rules missing.

Connor O'Brien: Root cause looks like `airgap-bundle-manifest.yaml` doesn’t include the dashboards/rules artifacts from `observability-pack` (only includes images + helm charts). Probably got dropped in the last refactor.

Dylan Brooks: I can take a look at the bundler config. It should include:
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/dashboards/*.json`
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/prometheus-rules/*.yaml`
And then installer should copy into the expected `/var/lib/redwood/observability/` path.

Chloe Martin: If we add raw dashboards, make sure we preserve folder structure so Grafana sidecar labels/folders still work (Runtime vs Control Plane vs Infra). Otherwise everything lands in “General”.

Diego Santos: Also ensure checksums update. Airgap customers verify SHA256 from the manifest.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed plan:
1) Patch installer bundle manifest to include dashboards + rules
2) Cut a hotfix PR + backport to release branch
3) Trigger airgap build + rerun onprem validation plan (at least: install, upgrade, confirm dashboards load)

Rafael Mendes: For severity: I’d call this a release-blocker for on-prem. Without dashboards the “minimum pack” story is broken.

Fatima Noor: Agree. For regulated customers, they specifically asked for turnkey dashboards in offline env. This is exactly the failure mode.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a CI step to the installer pipeline:
- unpack bundle
- `find . -name "*.json" | grep dashboards | wc -l` must be > 0
- and maybe validate presence of a known dashboard UID/title file

Elliot Price: Once fixed, can someone drop me the exact version + any user-facing notes? Docs currently say “dashboards included in offline bundle” so we should add a known-issue note for the broken versions + the fixed version.

Connor O'Brien: Dylan can you own the PR? I’ll coordinate the backport + release note.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. I’ll open a PR to `redwood-private-installer` updating the airgap manifest + post-install validation to fail loudly if dashboards directory is missing.

Diego Santos: Please add a preflight warning too. Something like: “observability artifacts not present in bundle; dashboards will be empty” so we don’t silently ship a broken pack.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a quick postinstall check that Grafana has loaded at least one dashboard ConfigMap? Even a simple “list ConfigMaps with grafana_dashboard=1 label” would help.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I’ll add a postinstall validation step:
`kubectl -n <ns> get cm -l grafana_dashboard=1 | grep redwood || exit 1`

Logan Wright: While you’re in there, make sure rules path is included too. We had a similar miss earlier where recording rules weren’t copied.

Dylan Brooks: Will do. I’ll verify both dashboard JSONs and rule YAMLs are bundled.

CI Bot: Build notification: nightly private-installer airgap job #482 succeeded (artifact uploaded).

Rafael Mendes: Nightly succeeded but doesn’t validate contents. Let’s not rely on that.

Connor O'Brien: Tracking: Dylan PR today, backport tomorrow AM, then Fatima to re-run the onprem airgap validation checklist.

Fatima Noor: I can rerun as soon as we have a new bundle. I’ll test:
- fresh airgap install
- upgrade from previous broken bundle -> fixed bundle
- confirm dashboards render and alerts present

Elliot Price: Please capture screenshots or at least the list of dashboards showing up. Helps for Support enablement.

Dylan Brooks: PR incoming in ~1 hour.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated with the PR link + the fixed build ID once it’s cut.

title: #eng-infra: 1746384109
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to land a default Prometheus TSDB sizing + retention rec for Private min pack. What are we comfortable shipping as "sane defaults" for typical footprints? Mostly concerned about PVC size + churn when folks have 15s scrapes.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Support keeps getting "Prometheus disk full" escalations, and we don’t have consistent guidance. Can we target 7d retention as default and document 15d/30d options?

Allison Grant: From standards side: minimum pack should be conservative on cardinality, but customers will still have node exporter + kube metrics + our runtime metrics. Default should prioritize stability over long retention. 7d time-based retention is fine, but we should also recommend `retention.size` as a guardrail.

Logan Wright: Agree. Also reminder: some Private installs use smaller clusters / single AZ w/ standard storage class. We should avoid recommending huge PVCs by default if we can.

Diego Santos: What are we calling "typical"? I’m seeing:
- small: 2–4 GPU nodes, <100 RPS
- medium: 8–20 GPU nodes, 100–500 RPS
- large: 50+ nodes, >500 RPS
Prometheus is mostly cluster metrics + runtime, not per-request.

Thomas Baird: Quick rule of thumb that’s worked: TSDB footprint ~ 1–3 bytes/sample on disk (varies), plus head/WAL overhead. In K8s installs, people underestimate churn (pods, containers, series churn). I’d budget 2x for head + compaction + WAL.

Sean Gallagher: Do we have any datapoints from our staging private cluster? Like actual series count + disk/day?

Sahana Iyer: From the smoke-test harness (single cluster, 3 serving pods, control plane, kube-state-metrics, node exporter) I saw ~180k active series once it settles. Disk growth was ~1.5–2.0GB/day at 15s scrapes w/ 7d retention, but that cluster is tiny.

Rafael Mendes: That’s helpful. Most customer clusters will have more nodes + more container metrics. Also some will turn on additional exporters (ingress-nginx, etc.).

Allison Grant: We should explicitly call out scrape interval expectations. If we’re assuming 15s and customer sets 5s, our sizing falls apart.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging-wise: helm values today default to 15s scrape in kube-prometheus-stack. We can document that changing it requires re-sizing PVC.

Thomas Baird: Another thing: enable `--storage.tsdb.wal-compression` by default. Helps w/ WAL size for write-heavy setups.

Logan Wright: +1 on WAL compression. Also I’m in favor of setting both:
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=7d`
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.size=80GB` (or similar)
So time-based retention doesn’t fill disks if there’s unexpected cardinality.

Sean Gallagher: What should the PVC be then? If we cap size at 80GB but PVC is 50Gi, we’re going to thrash earlier.

Thomas Baird: Prometheus needs free space for compaction and block writes. If you set retention.size near the PVC limit, you can still OOM/lock up on compaction.
Rule I use: retention.size <= ~60–70% of PVC. Rest is head/WAL/compaction buffer.

Rafael Mendes: Support reality: customers don’t want Prometheus consuming >200Gi unless we justify it.
Can we do defaults like:
- small: 50Gi PVC, 7d, retention.size 30Gi
- medium: 200Gi PVC, 15d, retention.size 120Gi
- large: 500Gi+, 15–30d depending on remote write
?

Allison Grant: That seems reasonable. For the minimum pack, we probably ship one default (small/medium-ish) and then provide a sizing table.

Diego Santos: I’d rather we ship a single default that won’t blow up on "medium". Maybe:
- default PVC: 100Gi
- default retention.time: 7d
- default retention.size: 60Gi
Then document: if you want 15d, go 200–300Gi.

Logan Wright: 100Gi default might be too big for some on-prem (local PVs are annoying), but too small for large clusters. Still probably the least-worst.

Sahana Iyer: FWIW, some air-gapped POCs have *tiny* storage classes. We got a question last week: “can I run this with 20Gi?”

Sean Gallagher: 20Gi feels like guaranteed pain unless you’re scraping almost nothing.

Thomas Baird: Yeah 20Gi will fill in a couple days once kube metrics are in. Also if storage is slow, compaction gets backed up.
We should add a note: “use SSD-backed storage; avoid networked HDD for Prometheus TSDB if possible.”

Rafael Mendes: Also: what about Grafana? People ask for "Grafana storage" too. It’s mostly sqlite + plugins. We can say 5–10Gi PVC is enough.

Allison Grant: +1. Grafana persistent volume mostly for dashboards/provisioning state. If dashboards are provisioned from configmaps, you can even run ephemeral, but let’s keep 10Gi default to be safe.

Connor O'Brien: In helm we already provision dashboards as configmaps. Grafana PVC is optional, but enabling persistence avoids losing user-created dashboards. Minimum pack probably shouldn’t assume custom dashboards though.

Sean Gallagher: OK, proposing we bake into doc + values:
Prometheus:
- PVC: 100Gi default
- retention.time: 7d
- retention.size: 60Gi
- wal-compression: true
And we publish a table (small/medium/large) with recommended PVC/retention.

Thomas Baird: I’d add one more knob: set requests/limits for Prometheus so it doesn’t get evicted under pressure. CPU spikes during compaction.
Maybe requests: 2 CPU / 8Gi mem, limits: 4 CPU / 16Gi mem for the default footprint.

Rafael Mendes: +1, we’ve seen OOMKills in customer clusters when compaction runs and memory is too low.

Sahana Iyer: In CI, Prometheus w/ 2Gi mem was flakey. 8Gi request stabilized it.

Allison Grant: Can we also include “cardinality guardrails” section: if series > X, you should reduce labels or drop high-churn metrics? That’ll help Support.

Logan Wright: Yes. Maybe a quick snippet to check series count:
`prometheus_tsdb_head_series`
And churn:
`rate(prometheus_tsdb_head_series_created_total[5m])`

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll update the sizing guidance draft with:
- default values
- sizing table (small/med/large)
- formulas + series/churn queries
- callouts about scrape interval + SSD storage

Connor O'Brien: Please also add a one-liner for installer preflight: warn if Prometheus PVC < 50Gi when min pack enabled.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and add "if disk usage >85% page" guidance + runbook link. That’s the common incident path.

Sean Gallagher: ack. I’ll also add that `retention.size` should be <70% of PVC to leave compaction headroom.

Thomas Baird: One nit: Gi vs GB confusion. In values, it’s Gi. In docs, call it out explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1. People misconfigure and then wonder why it’s tight.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, I’ll push an update today and drop link here for review.

Sean Gallagher: (later) Draft updates are in the doc: added table + default `100Gi / 7d / 60Gi`. Please comment if you think 100Gi is too aggressive for default installs.

Rafael Mendes: Reviewed. Looks good. I added a comment to emphasize remote_write as the path for >15d retention instead of bumping TSDB forever.

Logan Wright: +1 on remote_write note. Otherwise folks treat Prometheus as their long-term store.

Allison Grant: Left minor edits on wording + added the series/churn queries. Approved from standards POV.

Connor O'Brien: From packaging POV: OK. Preflight warning threshold at 50Gi makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: :shipit: I’ll proceed with the defaults + docs and we can tweak if staging noise comes back.

title: #eng-oncall: 1743531189
preview:
Connor O'Brien: seeing new overload panels light up in us-east. `breaker_open_rate` for hosted chat jumped to ~6% last 10m. anyone else looking?

incident-bot: Breaker state change: `hosted-chat/us-east/llama-70b` CLOSED -> OPEN (reason=scheduler_backlog_high). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/overload/overload-protection?var=region=us-east&var=model=llama-70b

Christine Wong: yep, watching. want to sanity check if this is real saturation vs us tripping the new signal wiring.

Rafael Mendes: +1. before we do anything, verify no false positives:
- runtime `prefill_q_depth` / `decode_q_depth`
- gateway shed rate vs 5xx/timeouts
- client retry behavior (don’t want a storm)

Aisha Bello: pulling dashboards now. On the overload view:
- `shed_rate{route=/v1/chat/completions}` peaked 4.8%
- `breaker_open_rate` correlates w/ `runtime_scheduler_backlog_p95` spike
- overall 5xx flat-ish, timeouts down a bit vs last burst :thinking_face:

Connor O'Brien: that’s… encouraging? do we know if this is just a single tenant hitting caps?

Christine Wong: check dimension by tenant_id (coarse). should be `tier_bucket` + `top_tenants` panel (limited cardinality).

Aisha Bello: top_tenants panel shows `banyan-search` at ~38% of requests on that model in the window. tier=SelfServe.

Rafael Mendes: ok so likely legit traffic spike. any indication breaker opened for enterprise tenants too?

Aisha Bello: looking at tier split:
- SelfServe shed ~6%
- Enterprise hosted shed ~0.2%
- Dedicated none (as expected)
so isolation seems to be working.

Connor O'Brien: what about false positive due to config threshold too low? what’s queue depth doing.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime metrics: `decode_queue_depth_avg` went 3 -> 19, `kv_cache_pressure` also climbed but not pegged. `tokens_per_sec_utilization` ~0.92 for ~7 min. looks like actual saturation.

Christine Wong: agreed. signal path seems consistent (queue depth -> backlog -> breaker). not seeing weird oscillation either.

Rafael Mendes: question is routing: are we successfully falling back for tenants that have fallback configured? if not, we may need routing owner.

Elena Popov: I can check routing logs quickly. any specific tenant with fallback?

Connor O'Brien: enterprise folks usually have `fallback_model=llama-70b-instruct-quant` or region failover. but this spike is self-serve, so maybe not many configured.

Aisha Bello: there *is* a small number of fallback attempts in the routing panel: `fallback_attempt_rate` ~0.4%, `fallback_success_rate` ~0.35%. Not zero.

Rafael Mendes: ok. but we had that recent issue where fallback didn’t trigger when breaker was open due to loop prevention being too strict. worth paging Mina/Peter?

Christine Wong: I’d page only if:
1) breaker open rate stays >5% for >15m AND
2) fallback_attempt_rate is near-zero while breaker is open OR errors show `overload_no_fallback_available` unexpectedly.

Elena Popov: quick grep from routing service (sampled):
```
reason=breaker_open action=fallback model=llama-70b -> llama-70b-compat region=us-west result=success
reason=breaker_open action=reject code=OVERLOAD_ADMISSION_SHED retry_after=2
```
not seeing `loop_prevented` spam.

Connor O'Brien: so seems healthy. any customer impact? support channel quiet?

Rafael Mendes: main thing is we don’t want the breaker to flap and confuse clients. are we setting Retry-After?

Christine Wong: header should be consistent now. but there was that ticket about missing Retry-After on some routes. Aisha can you spot-check sample responses in logs?

Aisha Bello: from gateway access logs (10 samples), all 503 overload responses had `retry_after_ms` field populated + `Retry-After: 2`. no 429s in this window.

Sofia Ivanova: also note: runtime latency p99 held steady (slightly up), but timeouts are lower than yesterday’s burst. shedding seems to be doing its job.

Connor O'Brien: ok. do we need to take action or just monitor?

Rafael Mendes: monitor + post update in #incidents if it grows. for eng-oncall: I’d avoid paging routing unless fallback metrics degrade.

Christine Wong: +1. maybe also verify breaker state transitions aren’t overly chatty. incident-bot only posted once.

text:
Connor O'Brien: seeing new overload panels light up in us-east. `breaker_open_rate` for hosted chat jumped to ~6% last 10m. anyone else looking?

incident-bot: Breaker state change: `hosted-chat/us-east/llama-70b` CLOSED -> OPEN (reason=scheduler_backlog_high). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/overload/overload-protection?var=region=us-east&var=model=llama-70b

Christine Wong: yep, watching. want to sanity check if this is real saturation vs us tripping the new signal wiring.

Rafael Mendes: +1. before we do anything, verify no false positives:
- runtime `prefill_q_depth` / `decode_q_depth`
- gateway shed rate vs 5xx/timeouts
- client retry behavior (don’t want a storm)

Aisha Bello: pulling dashboards now. On the overload view:
- `shed_rate{route=/v1/chat/completions}` peaked 4.8%
- `breaker_open_rate` correlates w/ `runtime_scheduler_backlog_p95` spike
- overall 5xx flat-ish, timeouts down a bit vs last burst :thinking_face:

Connor O'Brien: that’s… encouraging? do we know if this is just a single tenant hitting caps?

Christine Wong: check dimension by tenant_id (coarse). should be `tier_bucket` + `top_tenants` panel (limited cardinality).

Aisha Bello: top_tenants panel shows `banyan-search` at ~38% of requests on that model in the window. tier=SelfServe.

Rafael Mendes: ok so likely legit traffic spike. any indication breaker opened for enterprise tenants too?

Aisha Bello: looking at tier split:
- SelfServe shed ~6%
- Enterprise hosted shed ~0.2%
- Dedicated none (as expected)
so isolation seems to be working.

Connor O'Brien: what about false positive due to config threshold too low? what’s queue depth doing.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime metrics: `decode_queue_depth_avg` went 3 -> 19, `kv_cache_pressure` also climbed but not pegged. `tokens_per_sec_utilization` ~0.92 for ~7 min. looks like actual saturation.

Christine Wong: agreed. signal path seems consistent (queue depth -> backlog -> breaker). not seeing weird oscillation either.

Rafael Mendes: question is routing: are we successfully falling back for tenants that have fallback configured? if not, we may need routing owner.

Elena Popov: I can check routing logs quickly. any specific tenant with fallback?

Connor O'Brien: enterprise folks usually have `fallback_model=llama-70b-instruct-quant` or region failover. but this spike is self-serve, so maybe not many configured.

Aisha Bello: there *is* a small number of fallback attempts in the routing panel: `fallback_attempt_rate` ~0.4%, `fallback_success_rate` ~0.35%. Not zero.

Rafael Mendes: ok. but we had that recent issue where fallback didn’t trigger when breaker was open due to loop prevention being too strict. worth paging Mina/Peter?

Christine Wong: I’d page only if:
1) breaker open rate stays >5% for >15m AND
2) fallback_attempt_rate is near-zero while breaker is open OR errors show `overload_no_fallback_available` unexpectedly.

Elena Popov: quick grep from routing service (sampled):
```
reason=breaker_open action=fallback model=llama-70b -> llama-70b-compat region=us-west result=success
reason=breaker_open action=reject code=OVERLOAD_ADMISSION_SHED retry_after=2
```
not seeing `loop_prevented` spam.

Connor O'Brien: so seems healthy. any customer impact? support channel quiet?

Rafael Mendes: main thing is we don’t want the breaker to flap and confuse clients. are we setting Retry-After?

Christine Wong: header should be consistent now. but there was that ticket about missing Retry-After on some routes. Aisha can you spot-check sample responses in logs?

Aisha Bello: from gateway access logs (10 samples), all 503 overload responses had `retry_after_ms` field populated + `Retry-After: 2`. no 429s in this window.

Sofia Ivanova: also note: runtime latency p99 held steady (slightly up), but timeouts are lower than yesterday’s burst. shedding seems to be doing its job.

Connor O'Brien: ok. do we need to take action or just monitor?

Rafael Mendes: monitor + post update in #incidents if it grows. for eng-oncall: I’d avoid paging routing unless fallback metrics degrade.

Christine Wong: +1. maybe also verify breaker state transitions aren’t overly chatty. incident-bot only posted once.

incident-bot: Breaker state change: `hosted-chat/us-east/llama-70b` OPEN -> HALF_OPEN (reason=recovery_probe). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/overload/overload-protection?var=region=us-east&var=model=llama-70b

Aisha Bello: it’s half-open now. `probe_success_rate` looks good (0.98).

Connor O'Brien: nice. if it closes cleanly, we’re good.

Peter Holtz: catching up. agree no need to page Mina unless we see fallback not engaging. if you see `fallback_blocked_loop_prevention` in routing logs, ping me.

Christine Wong: thanks Peter. will keep an eye.

incident-bot: Breaker state change: `hosted-chat/us-east/llama-70b` HALF_OPEN -> CLOSED (reason=recovered). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/overload/overload-protection?var=region=us-east&var=model=llama-70b

Rafael Mendes: cool. can we capture a quick note for tuning? (thresholds seem ok; isolation worked)

Aisha Bello: I’ll drop a short summary in the tuning sheet later today: peak shed 4.8%, no enterprise impact, p99 stable, Retry-After present.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: thanks all. no page to routing owner for now.
